title: Short title, commencement and application
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: This Act may be called the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita, 2023. It shall come into force on such date as the Central Government may appoint by notification. Every person shall be liable for offences committed within India. The provisions also apply to offences committed outside India by Indian citizens, persons on Indian registered ships or aircraft, and persons targeting computer resources located in India.
simpleExplanation: This section gives the name of the law, explains when it becomes effective, and states who can be prosecuted under it, including certain offences committed outside India.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An Indian citizen commits murder in another country and later returns to India. He may be tried in India under this Sanhita.

faqs.question: Does BNS apply outside India?
faqs.answer: Yes, in specified situations involving Indian citizens, Indian-registered vessels or aircraft, and cyber offences targeting India.

title: Definitions
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: Defines important terms used throughout the Sanhita including act, animal, child, counterfeit, Court, death, dishonestly, document, fraudulently, gender, good faith, Government, harbour, injury, illegal, Judge, life, local law, movable property, offence, person, public servant, valuable security, vessel, voluntarily, wrongful gain and wrongful loss.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the meanings of important legal terms that are used throughout the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A cheque is considered a document.
A company is treated as a person under the Sanhita.

faqs.question: Why are definitions important?
faqs.answer: They ensure that legal terms have a uniform meaning throughout the law.

title: General explanations
chapter: Preliminary
officialText: Provides rules for interpreting offences, punishments and illustrations throughout the Sanhita. Clarifies concepts such as common intention, joint liability, criminal acts committed by several persons, possession through servants or spouses, and acts done partly by omission.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how the provisions of the Sanhita should be interpreted and applied in practice.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Several persons acting with a common intention may all be liable for the same offence.
A person may be criminally responsible for an offence committed partly through an omission.

faqs.question: What is common intention?
faqs.answer: It means several persons share the same criminal objective and act together to achieve it.

title: Punishments
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: The punishments under this Sanhita are death, imprisonment for life, rigorous or simple imprisonment, forfeiture of property, fine and community service.
simpleExplanation: This section lists all types of punishments that courts can impose under the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
punishment: Defines available punishments; does not prescribe punishment for a specific offence.
examples: A court may order community service for offences where such punishment is permitted.

faqs.question: What is new in BNS compared to IPC?
faqs.answer: Community service has been expressly included as a form of punishment.

title: Commutation of sentence
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: The appropriate Government may commute any punishment under this Sanhita to another punishment in accordance with the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023.
simpleExplanation: This section allows the Central or State Government to reduce or substitute certain punishments according to law.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A sentence of imprisonment may be commuted as permitted under law.

faqs.question: Who can commute a sentence?
faqs.answer: The appropriate Central or State Government, depending on the nature of the offence.

title: Fractions of terms of punishment
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: For calculating fractions of terms of punishment, imprisonment for life shall be reckoned as equivalent to imprisonment for twenty years unless otherwise provided.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how life imprisonment is treated when calculating fractions of a sentence.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: For certain calculations, life imprisonment is treated as twenty years.

faqs.question: Does life imprisonment always mean 20 years?
faqs.answer: No. The section applies only for calculation purposes unless another provision says otherwise.

title: Sentence may be wholly or partly rigorous or simple
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: Where an offence is punishable with imprisonment of either description, the court may direct that the imprisonment be wholly rigorous, wholly simple, or partly rigorous and partly simple.
simpleExplanation: This section gives courts discretion to decide the nature of imprisonment imposed.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A court may order six months rigorous imprisonment followed by six months simple imprisonment.

faqs.question: What is rigorous imprisonment?
faqs.answer: It involves imprisonment with hard labour.

title: Amount of fine, liability in default of payment of fine, etc.
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: Provides rules regarding fines, imprisonment for non-payment of fines, imprisonment in default of community service, and recovery of unpaid fines.
simpleExplanation: This section explains how fines are imposed, what happens if they are not paid, and how unpaid fines may be recovered.
punishment: Imprisonment in default of payment of fine or non-performance of community service as specified by the court and subject to statutory limits.
examples: A person sentenced to a fine may be imprisoned if the fine is not paid.

faqs.question: Can a fine be recovered after sentencing?
faqs.answer: Yes, the law permits recovery of unpaid fines within the prescribed period.

title: Limit of punishment of offence made up of several offences
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: Provides that where an offence consists of several parts or falls under multiple definitions, the offender shall not ordinarily receive multiple punishments for the same act unless expressly provided.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents excessive punishment when a single act technically amounts to multiple offences.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person who causes hurt through multiple blows is punished for the overall offence and not separately for every blow.

faqs.question: Can one act result in multiple punishments?
faqs.answer: Generally no, unless the law specifically allows it.

title: Punishment where guilt of one of several offences is doubtful
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: Where a person is found guilty of one among several offences but it is doubtful which offence has been committed, the offender shall be punished with the lowest punishment prescribed among those offences if the punishments differ.
simpleExplanation: If the court is unsure which of several offences was committed, the accused receives the benefit of the lesser punishment.
punishment: Lowest applicable punishment among the offences proved.
examples: Where evidence establishes guilt but leaves doubt between two offences carrying different punishments, the lesser punishment applies.

faqs.question: Why is the lower punishment applied?
faqs.answer: Because criminal law gives the benefit of doubt to the accused.

title: Solitary confinement
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: Whenever any person is convicted of an offence for which the Court has power to sentence him to rigorous imprisonment, the Court may order that the offender be kept in solitary confinement for portions of the imprisonment not exceeding three months in total, subject to statutory limits.
simpleExplanation: The court may order solitary confinement as part of a rigorous imprisonment sentence, but only within specified limits.
punishment: Solitary confinement up to 1 month if imprisonment does not exceed 6 months; up to 2 months if imprisonment exceeds 6 months but not 1 year; up to 3 months if imprisonment exceeds 1 year.
examples: A person sentenced to 2 years of rigorous imprisonment may be ordered to undergo up to 3 months of solitary confinement.

faqs.question: Can solitary confinement be imposed in every case?
faqs.answer: No. It can only be imposed where rigorous imprisonment is authorized.

title: Limit of solitary confinement
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: Solitary confinement shall not exceed fourteen days at a time and must be separated by intervals of equal duration. Where imprisonment exceeds three months, solitary confinement shall not exceed seven days in any one month.
simpleExplanation: This section limits how long and how frequently solitary confinement can be imposed.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: An offender cannot be kept continuously in solitary confinement for several months.

faqs.question: Can solitary confinement be continuous?
faqs.answer: No. The law requires intervals between periods of solitary confinement.

title: Enhanced punishment for certain offences after previous conviction
chapter: Chapter II - Of Punishments
officialText: A person previously convicted of specified offences under Chapter X or Chapter XVII and subsequently convicted again of a similar offence may be subjected to enhanced punishment.
simpleExplanation: Habitual offenders who repeatedly commit certain serious offences can receive harsher punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to 10 years.
examples: A repeat offender convicted again of a qualifying property offence may receive enhanced punishment.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: To deter repeat offenders and protect society from habitual criminal conduct.

title: Act done by a person bound, or by mistake of fact believing himself bound, by law
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not by reason of a mistake of law in good faith believes himself to be, bound by law to do it.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for an act done because the law required it, or because he honestly believed the law required it due to a mistake of fact.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A soldier fires on a violent mob under lawful orders.

faqs.question: Does a mistake of law provide protection?
faqs.answer: No. Only a genuine mistake of fact may provide protection.

title: Act of Judge when acting judicially
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a Judge when acting judicially in the exercise of any power which is, or which in good faith he believes to be, given to him by law.
simpleExplanation: Judges are protected from criminal liability when performing judicial functions in good faith.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A Judge passing a lawful sentence is protected under this section.

faqs.question: Does this protection apply to all acts of a Judge?
faqs.answer: Only acts performed judicially and in good faith.

title: Act done pursuant to judgment or order of Court
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing done pursuant to a judgment or order of a Court is an offence if done while the judgment or order remains in force and the person acts in good faith believing the Court had jurisdiction.
simpleExplanation: A person carrying out a court order is generally protected from criminal liability.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A court officer executing an arrest warrant is protected.

faqs.question: What if the court later turns out to lack jurisdiction?
faqs.answer: Protection remains if the person acted in good faith.

title: Act done by a person justified, or by mistake of fact believing himself justified, by law
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who is justified by law, or who by reason of a mistake of fact and not mistake of law, in good faith believes himself justified by law.
simpleExplanation: A person acting under lawful authority or a genuine belief based on facts is protected.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A citizen apprehending a person whom he reasonably believes committed murder.

faqs.question: What kind of mistake is protected?
faqs.answer: A mistake of fact made in good faith.

title: Accident in doing a lawful act
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by accident or misfortune, without criminal intention or knowledge, while doing a lawful act in a lawful manner by lawful means with proper care and caution.
simpleExplanation: An accidental act committed while acting lawfully and carefully is not a crime.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A tool accidentally slips from a worker's hand despite reasonable care and causes injury.

faqs.question: Is negligence protected?
faqs.answer: No. The act must have been done with proper care and caution.

title: Act likely to cause harm, but done without criminal intent, and to prevent other harm
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: An act done in good faith without criminal intent to prevent or avoid greater harm is not an offence merely because it is likely to cause some harm.
simpleExplanation: The law may excuse a harmful act if it was done honestly to prevent a greater danger.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Demolishing a building to stop a large fire from spreading.

faqs.question: What is the key requirement?
faqs.answer: The act must be done in good faith and to prevent a greater harm.

title: Act of a child under seven years of age
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child under seven years of age.
simpleExplanation: Children below seven years are considered incapable of committing a criminal offence.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A six-year-old child who causes damage cannot be prosecuted under criminal law.

faqs.question: Can a child below seven be held criminally liable?
faqs.answer: No. Such a child is absolutely exempt from criminal liability.

title: Act of a child above seven and under twelve years of age of immature understanding
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a child above seven years of age and under twelve years of age who has not attained sufficient maturity of understanding to judge the nature and consequences of his conduct.
simpleExplanation: A child between seven and twelve years is not criminally liable if the child lacks sufficient maturity to understand the consequences of the act.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A nine-year-old child unknowingly causes damage without understanding its consequences.

faqs.question: Is every child below twelve exempt from criminal liability?
faqs.answer: No. The court must determine whether the child had sufficient maturity of understanding.

title: Act of a person of unsound mind
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done by a person who, at the time of doing it, by reason of unsoundness of mind, is incapable of knowing the nature of the act, or that what he is doing is either wrong or contrary to law.
simpleExplanation: A person suffering from mental incapacity is not criminally liable if unable to understand the nature or wrongfulness of the act.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person suffering from severe psychosis commits an act without understanding its nature.

faqs.question: Does every mental illness provide a defence?
faqs.answer: No. The illness must render the person incapable of understanding the act or its wrongfulness.

title: Act of a person incapable of judgment by reason of intoxication caused against his will
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence committed by a person who, because of intoxication administered without his knowledge or against his will, is incapable of understanding the nature of the act or that it is wrong or contrary to law.
simpleExplanation: A person is protected when intoxication was involuntary and prevented understanding of the act.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person's drink is secretly drugged, causing loss of judgment.

faqs.question: Does voluntary intoxication qualify?
faqs.answer: No. The intoxication must have been caused without the person's knowledge or against their will.

title: Offence requiring a particular intent or knowledge committed by one who is intoxicated
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: A person committing an act while intoxicated is treated as having the same knowledge as if not intoxicated, unless the intoxication was administered without his knowledge or against his will.
simpleExplanation: Voluntary intoxication generally does not excuse criminal responsibility where knowledge or intent is required.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person voluntarily intoxicated cannot avoid liability by claiming drunkenness.

faqs.question: Can intoxication be used as a defence?
faqs.answer: Only when the intoxication was involuntary.

title: Act not intended and not known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt, done by consent
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: An act not intended or known to be likely to cause death or grievous hurt is not an offence when done with the consent of a person above eighteen years of age who agrees to suffer the harm or risk.
simpleExplanation: Adults can legally consent to certain risks or harms that are not intended to cause death or grievous injury.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Two adults participate in a lawful sporting activity and one suffers a minor injury.

faqs.question: Can consent justify serious injury?
faqs.answer: No. The protection does not extend to acts intended or known to cause death or grievous hurt.

title: Act not intended to cause death, done by consent in good faith for person's benefit
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence by reason of harm caused to a person for whose benefit it is done in good faith, with that person's consent, provided death is not intended.
simpleExplanation: Acts done in good faith for a person's benefit, such as medical treatment, may be lawful when consent is given.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A surgeon performs a risky operation with the patient's consent.

faqs.question: Why are medical operations protected?
faqs.answer: Because they are performed in good faith for the patient's benefit.

title: Act done in good faith for benefit of child or person of unsound mind, by or by consent of guardian
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Acts done in good faith for the benefit of a child under twelve years or a person of unsound mind are protected when done with the consent of a guardian, subject to specified limitations.
simpleExplanation: Guardians may consent to beneficial acts on behalf of children or persons of unsound mind, within legal limits.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A guardian consents to surgery necessary for a child's health.

faqs.question: Can a guardian consent to anything?
faqs.answer: No. The section excludes intentional causing of death and certain serious harms.

title: Consent known to be given under fear or misconception
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Consent is invalid if obtained under fear of injury, misconception of fact, from a person incapable of understanding due to unsoundness of mind or intoxication, or from a child under twelve years unless the context indicates otherwise.
simpleExplanation: Consent must be free, informed and given by a competent person.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Consent obtained through threats is not legally valid.

faqs.question: What makes consent invalid?
faqs.answer: Fear, fraud, misconception, intoxication, unsoundness of mind or lack of legal capacity.

title: Exclusion of acts which are offences independently of harm caused
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: The exceptions relating to consent do not apply to acts that are offences independently of the harm caused, such as acts specifically criminalized by law.
simpleExplanation: Consent cannot legalize conduct that is inherently criminal.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person's consent cannot justify an act that is independently prohibited by law.

faqs.question: Can consent always be used as a defence?
faqs.answer: No. Some offences remain crimes regardless of consent.

title: Act done in good faith for benefit of a person without consent
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: An act done in good faith for a person's benefit is not an offence even without consent when it is impossible to obtain consent in time and the circumstances require immediate action, subject to statutory limitations.
simpleExplanation: Emergency actions taken to save or benefit a person may be lawful even without consent.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A surgeon performs an emergency operation on an unconscious accident victim.
A person rescues a child from a burning building despite the risk of injury.

faqs.question: When can consent be dispensed with?
faqs.answer: When obtaining consent is impossible and immediate action is necessary for the person's benefit.

title: Communication made in good faith
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: No communication made in good faith is an offence by reason of any harm to the person to whom it is made, if it is made for the benefit of that person.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for honestly communicating information intended for another person's benefit, even if it causes distress or harm.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A doctor honestly informs a patient about a terminal illness.

faqs.question: Does hurtful information become an offence?
faqs.answer: No, if it is communicated in good faith for the person's benefit.

title: Act to which a person is compelled by threats
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Except murder and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence if done under threats creating a reasonable apprehension of instant death.
simpleExplanation: A person forced to commit an offence due to an immediate threat of death may be excused from criminal liability in certain cases.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person forced at gunpoint to assist criminals may be protected under this section.

faqs.question: Does this defence apply to murder?
faqs.answer: No. Murder and certain offences against the State are excluded.

title: Act causing slight harm
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Nothing is an offence by reason that it causes, or is intended or known to cause, any harm if that harm is so slight that no person of ordinary sense and temper would complain of it.
simpleExplanation: The law ignores trivial acts that cause only insignificant harm.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: Accidentally brushing against someone in a crowded place.

faqs.question: What is considered slight harm?
faqs.answer: Harm so minor that a reasonable person would not complain about it.

title: Things done in private defence
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: Nothing is an offence which is done in the exercise of the right of private defence.
simpleExplanation: Acts done while lawfully defending oneself or another person may not amount to an offence.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person pushes away an attacker to protect himself.

faqs.question: Is every act of self-defence protected?
faqs.answer: Only acts within the legal limits of private defence.

title: Right of private defence of body and of property
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: Every person has a right to defend his own body, the body of another person, and property against specified offences including theft, robbery, mischief and criminal trespass.
simpleExplanation: The law allows a person to protect people and property from unlawful attacks and offences.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A homeowner prevents a burglar from stealing property.

faqs.question: Can a person defend someone else's property?
faqs.answer: Yes, within the limits prescribed by law.

title: Right of private defence against act of a person of unsound mind, etc.
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: A person has the same right of private defence against acts committed by children, persons of unsound mind, intoxicated persons or persons acting under a mistake as he would have if the act were an offence.
simpleExplanation: The right of private defence exists even if the aggressor is legally incapable of committing an offence.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person may defend himself against an attack by an insane individual.

faqs.question: Does self-defence depend on the attacker's criminal liability?
faqs.answer: No. The right exists regardless of the attacker's legal capacity.

title: Acts against which there is no right of private defence
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: There is no right of private defence against certain acts of public servants acting in good faith, against acts done under their direction, or where there is sufficient time to seek protection from public authorities.
simpleExplanation: The right of private defence is not unlimited and cannot be used in certain situations.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person cannot lawfully resist a public servant acting in good faith under lawful authority.

faqs.question: Can excessive force be used in private defence?
faqs.answer: No. The harm inflicted must not exceed what is necessary.

title: When right of private defence of body extends to causing death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: The right of private defence extends to causing death when faced with specified serious offences such as assaults causing reasonable apprehension of death, grievous hurt, rape, kidnapping, wrongful confinement, or acid attacks.
simpleExplanation: In certain grave situations, a person may use force even to the extent of causing the attacker's death.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A victim resists an attempted rape using deadly force.

faqs.question: When can deadly force be used in self-defence?
faqs.answer: Only in the serious situations specifically listed in the section.

title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: If the offence does not fall within the categories specified in section 38, the right of private defence extends only to causing harm other than death.
simpleExplanation: In less serious situations, self-defence allows only reasonable non-fatal force.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person restrains a minor attacker without using deadly force.

faqs.question: Can death be caused in every self-defence situation?
faqs.answer: No. Only situations covered under section 38 permit that extent of force.

title: Commencement and continuance of right of private defence of body
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: The right of private defence of the body begins as soon as a reasonable apprehension of danger arises and continues as long as such apprehension continues.
simpleExplanation: Self-defence starts when there is a reasonable threat and ends when the threat is over.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person may act in self-defence before being physically attacked if danger is imminent.

faqs.question: When does the right of private defence end?
faqs.answer: It ends when the reasonable apprehension of danger ceases.

title: Communication made in good faith
chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: No communication made in good faith is an offence by reason of any harm to the person to whom it is made, if it is made for the benefit of that person.
simpleExplanation: A person is not criminally liable for honestly communicating information intended for another person's benefit, even if it causes distress or harm.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A doctor honestly informs a patient about a terminal illness.

faqs.question: Does hurtful information become an offence?
faqs.answer: No, if it is communicated in good faith for the person's benefit.

chapter: Chapter III - General Exceptions
officialText: Except murder and offences against the State punishable with death, nothing is an offence if done under threats creating a reasonable apprehension of instant death.
simpleExplanation: A person forced to commit an offence due to an immediate threat of death may be excused from criminal liability in certain cases.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person forced at gunpoint to assist criminals may be protected under this section.

faqs.answer: No. Murder and certain offences against the State are excluded.

title: When right of private defence of property extends to causing death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: The right of private defence of property extends to causing death when defending against robbery, house-breaking after sunset and before sunrise, mischief by fire or explosive substance to a dwelling or place of custody of property, and certain aggravated forms of theft, mischief or house-trespass.
simpleExplanation: In particularly serious attacks against property, the law allows a person to use force even to the extent of causing death.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A homeowner confronts an armed robber during a robbery.

faqs.question: Can deadly force be used to protect property?
faqs.answer: Only in the specific situations listed in this section.

title: When such right extends to causing any harm other than death
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: Where the offence is theft, mischief or criminal trespass not falling within section 41, the right of private defence extends only to causing harm other than death.
simpleExplanation: For less serious offences against property, only reasonable non-fatal force may be used.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A shopkeeper restrains a petty thief without using deadly force.

faqs.question: Can death be caused in every property offence?
faqs.answer: No. Only the situations mentioned in section 41 permit that level of force.

title: Commencement and continuance of right of private defence of property
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: The right of private defence of property begins when a reasonable apprehension of danger to property arises and continues for the periods specified depending on the nature of the offence.
simpleExplanation: This section explains when the right to defend property starts and when it ends.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: The right continues during an ongoing robbery until the danger ends.

faqs.question: When does property defence end?
faqs.answer: It ends when the threat ceases or the property is recovered, depending on the offence.

title: Right of private defence against deadly assault when there is risk of harm to innocent person
chapter: Chapter III - Of Right of Private Defence
officialText: If a person cannot effectively exercise the right of private defence against an assault causing reasonable apprehension of death without risking harm to an innocent person, the right extends to running that risk.
simpleExplanation: A defender may be protected even when defending himself creates unavoidable risk to innocent persons.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A person attacked by a violent mob acts in self-defence despite unavoidable risk to bystanders.

faqs.question: Can self-defence justify accidental harm to innocent persons?
faqs.answer: Yes, where such risk is unavoidable while lawfully exercising private defence.

title: Abetment of a thing
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: A person abets the doing of a thing if he instigates another person, engages in a conspiracy for its commission, or intentionally aids its commission.
simpleExplanation: A person commits abetment by encouraging, planning, or helping another person commit an offence.
punishment: Punishable according to the offence abetted and related provisions.
examples: A persuades B to commit theft.
A provides tools to help B commit burglary.

faqs.question: What are the forms of abetment?
faqs.answer: Instigation, conspiracy and intentional aid.

title: Abettor
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: A person abets an offence if he abets its commission or the commission of an act which would be an offence if committed by a person capable of committing it with the same intention or knowledge.
simpleExplanation: This section defines who is considered an abettor and clarifies the scope of criminal liability.
punishment: Punishable according to provisions relating to abetment.
examples: A persuades a child to commit an act which would be an offence if committed by an adult.

faqs.question: Can a person be guilty of abetment even if the principal offender is not?
faqs.answer: Yes, in certain circumstances.

title: Abetment in India of offences outside India
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: A person in India who abets the commission of an act outside India which would constitute an offence if committed in India is deemed to have abetted an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person in India can be punished for helping commit certain offences abroad.
punishment: Punishable as provided for abetment.
examples: A person in India encourages another person abroad to commit murder.

faqs.question: Can abetment apply to foreign offences?
faqs.answer: Yes, where the act would be an offence if committed in India.

title: Abetment outside India for offence in India
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: A person outside India who abets the commission in India of an act which would constitute an offence if committed in India commits abetment within the meaning of this Sanhita.
simpleExplanation: A person located outside India may be liable for encouraging or assisting offences committed in India.
punishment: Punishable as provided for abetment.
examples: A person abroad directs another person to commit a crime in India.

faqs.question: Can foreign residents be liable under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they abet offences committed in India.

title: Punishment of abetment if the act abetted is committed in consequence and where no express provision is made
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Where the act abetted is committed as a consequence of the abetment and no express provision exists for punishment, the abettor shall be punished with the punishment provided for the offence.
simpleExplanation: If a crime is committed because of the abetment, the abettor is generally punished like the principal offender unless another provision applies.
punishment: Same punishment as provided for the offence abetted.
examples: A instigates B to commit theft and B commits the theft.

faqs.question: Can an abettor receive the same punishment as the offender?
faqs.answer: Yes, where the law provides and no special provision applies.

title: Punishment of abetment if person abetted does act with different intention from that of abettor
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Where an act is committed with a different intention or knowledge from that of the abettor, the abettor is punishable with the punishment provided for the offence that would have been committed if the act had been done with the intention or knowledge of the abettor.
simpleExplanation: An abettor's liability is generally determined by his own intention rather than the different intention of the person who commits the act.
punishment: Punishment corresponding to the offence intended by the abettor.
examples: A instigates B to cause hurt, but B acts with a more serious intention.

faqs.question: Is the abettor responsible for intentions he never had?
faqs.answer: Generally, liability is based on the abettor's own intention or knowledge.

title: Liability of abettor when one act is abetted and a different act is done
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: When an act is abetted and a different act is done, the abettor is liable for the act actually done if it was a probable consequence of the abetment.
simpleExplanation: If someone encourages one crime but a related crime occurs as a likely result, the abettor may still be held responsible.
punishment: Punishment applicable to the offence that was the probable consequence of the abetment.
examples: A instigates B to assault C, and B causes grievous hurt that was a likely consequence.

faqs.question: Can an abettor be liable for a different offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the offence committed was a probable consequence of the abetment.

title: Abettor liable when effect caused is different from that intended
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: An abettor is liable where the act abetted causes a different effect from that intended, provided the effect caused was a probable consequence of the act.
simpleExplanation: An abettor cannot escape liability merely because the result was different from what was expected.
punishment: Punishment according to the offence resulting from the probable consequence.
examples: A abets causing hurt, but death results as a foreseeable consequence.

faqs.question: Must the exact result be intended?
faqs.answer: No. Liability may arise if the actual result was a probable consequence.

title: Liability of abettor for cumulative punishment
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Where an abettor is liable for both the act abetted and the act actually committed, punishment may be imposed for both offences.
simpleExplanation: An abettor may face punishment for multiple offences arising from the same abetment.
punishment: Punishment for both offences as permitted by law.
examples: An abettor may be punished for the intended offence and the resulting offence.

faqs.question: Can multiple punishments be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes, where the law permits liability for multiple offences.

title: Abettor present when offence is committed
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Whenever an abettor is present when the offence is committed, he is deemed to have committed the offence by such abetment.
simpleExplanation: An abettor who is physically present during the commission of the offence is treated more seriously by law.
punishment: Punishment prescribed for the offence committed.
examples: A encourages B to commit theft and remains present during the theft.

faqs.question: Why is presence important?
faqs.answer: Presence demonstrates active participation and support for the offence.

title: Abetment of offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life if offence not committed
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Abetment of an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life is punishable even if the offence is not committed.
simpleExplanation: Encouraging or assisting very serious crimes is itself punishable, even when the crime does not occur.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine; enhanced punishment if harm results.
examples: A persuades B to commit murder, but B refuses.

faqs.question: Can abetment be punished even if the crime never happens?
faqs.answer: Yes, for serious offences punishable with death or life imprisonment.

title: Abetment of offence punishable with imprisonment if offence not committed
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Abetment of an offence punishable with imprisonment is punishable even when the offence is not committed and no express provision exists.
simpleExplanation: A person may be punished merely for encouraging a crime, even if the crime never occurs.
punishment: Imprisonment up to one-fourth of the maximum term prescribed for the offence, or fine, or both.
examples: A persuades B to commit theft, but B does not act.

faqs.question: Does the crime need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. Abetment itself may be punishable.

title: Abetting commission of offence by the public or by more than ten persons
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Whoever abets the commission of an offence by the public generally or by more than ten persons is punishable.
simpleExplanation: Encouraging a crowd or large group to commit offences is treated as a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A publicly incites a crowd to commit acts of violence.

faqs.question: Why is public abetment treated seriously?
faqs.answer: Because it can lead to large-scale public disorder and harm.

title: Concealing design to commit offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: A person who intentionally conceals a plan to commit an offence punishable with death or imprisonment for life may be punished.
simpleExplanation: Knowing about a serious crime and intentionally hiding the information can itself be a crime.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years where the offence is committed; lesser punishment if not committed.
examples: A knows of a murder conspiracy and deliberately hides the information.

faqs.question: Is silence always punishable?
faqs.answer: No. There must be intentional concealment of the criminal design.

title: Public servant concealing design to commit offence which it is his duty to prevent
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: A public servant who intentionally conceals a criminal design that it is his duty to prevent commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Public servants have a legal duty to prevent offences and can be punished for deliberately hiding criminal plans.
punishment: Varies according to the offence intended and whether it is committed.
examples: A police officer conceals information about a planned terrorist attack.

faqs.question: Why is the punishment stricter for public servants?
faqs.answer: Because they have a legal duty to prevent and report offences.

title: Concealing design to commit offence punishable with imprisonment
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Whoever intending to facilitate or knowing it likely to facilitate the commission of an offence punishable with imprisonment voluntarily conceals the existence of a design to commit such offence is punishable.
simpleExplanation: A person who hides plans for a crime may be punished if the concealment helps the crime occur.
punishment: Punishment varies according to whether the offence is committed and the severity of the offence.
examples: A learns of a planned burglary and deliberately conceals the information from authorities.

faqs.question: Must the crime actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Concealment itself may be punishable.

title: Criminal Conspiracy
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: When two or more persons agree to do, or cause to be done, an illegal act or a legal act by illegal means, such agreement is designated a criminal conspiracy. Except for agreements to commit an offence, some overt act in pursuance of the agreement is required.
simpleExplanation: A criminal conspiracy occurs when two or more people agree to commit a crime or achieve a lawful objective through unlawful means.
punishment: Defined under Section 62.
examples: A and B agree to counterfeit currency.
A and B agree to obtain government contracts through bribery.

faqs.question: Is mere agreement enough?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the agreement is to commit an offence. Otherwise, an overt act is generally required.

title: Punishment for Criminal Conspiracy
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Persons who are parties to a criminal conspiracy are punishable according to the nature of the conspiracy and the offence intended.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for persons involved in criminal conspiracies.
punishment: Where conspiracy is to commit a serious offence, punishment may be the same as for abetment. In other cases, imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Persons conspiring to commit murder may face severe punishment.
Persons conspiring to commit a minor unlawful act may face lesser punishment.

faqs.question: Is conspiracy punishable even if the crime is not completed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The conspiracy itself is punishable.

title: Attempt to Commit Offences Punishable with Imprisonment for Life or Other Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter IV - Of Abetment, Criminal Conspiracy and Attempt
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment and does any act towards its commission shall be punished where no express provision exists for such attempt.
simpleExplanation: A person may be punished for trying to commit a crime even if the crime is not completed.
punishment: Up to one-half of the longest term of imprisonment provided for the offence, or fine, or both; where life imprisonment is prescribed, imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A attempts to commit robbery but is caught before taking property.

faqs.question: What is an attempt?
faqs.answer: An act done with criminal intent that moves beyond preparation towards committing the offence.

title: Rape
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Defines rape and specifies circumstances under which sexual acts constitute rape, including lack of consent, consent obtained through fear, deception, incapacity, or where the victim is under the prescribed age.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the offence of rape and identifies situations in which consent is absent or legally invalid.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under Section 65 and related provisions.
examples: Sexual intercourse without consent.
Intercourse obtained through threats or coercion.

faqs.question: Can consent obtained by fear be valid?
faqs.answer: No. Consent obtained by fear, coercion or deception is invalid.

title: Punishment for Rape
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Prescribes punishment for the offence of rape.
simpleExplanation: Any person convicted of rape is subject to severe imprisonment and fine.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years, extendable to imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: A person convicted under Section 64 is punished under this section.

faqs.question: Can a court impose life imprisonment?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on the facts and gravity of the offence.

title: Punishment for Causing Death or Persistent Vegetative State of Victim
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Provides punishment where rape results in death of the victim or causes the victim to be in a persistent vegetative state.
simpleExplanation: The law imposes enhanced punishment where rape causes death or permanent vegetative condition.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment of not less than 20 years extending to life imprisonment, or death.
examples: A rape victim dies due to injuries inflicted during the offence.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes, in appropriate cases under this section.

title: Sexual Intercourse by Husband Upon Wife During Separation
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Punishes sexual intercourse by a husband with his wife without her consent while they are living separately under a decree, custom or otherwise.
simpleExplanation: A husband may be punished for non-consensual sexual intercourse with his wife while they are living separately.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A separated husband forces sexual intercourse on his wife.

faqs.question: Does separation matter?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically applies where the spouses are living separately.

title: Sexual Intercourse by Person in Authority
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Punishes sexual intercourse by persons in authority or fiduciary positions who abuse their position to induce or seduce a woman.
simpleExplanation: Persons holding positions of authority cannot exploit their position for sexual relations.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment of 5 to 10 years and fine.
examples: A prison official exploits a detainee for sexual purposes.

faqs.question: Why is abuse of authority treated differently?
faqs.answer: Because the victim may be vulnerable due to the offender's position.

title: Sexual Intercourse by Employing Deceitful Means
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever, by deceitful means or making a false promise to marry without intention of fulfilling it, has sexual intercourse with a woman shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: Obtaining sexual relations through fraudulent promises or deception is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A person falsely promises marriage solely to obtain sexual consent.

faqs.question: Is every broken promise to marry a crime?
faqs.answer: No. The promise must have been false from the beginning and intended to deceive.

title: Gang Rape
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Where a woman is raped by one or more persons constituting a group or acting in furtherance of a common intention, each person is deemed to have committed gang rape.
simpleExplanation: All participants in a gang rape are equally responsible, whether or not each individually committed the sexual act.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 20 years, extendable to life imprisonment, and fine.
examples: Several persons acting together sexually assault a woman.

faqs.question: Are all members of the group liable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Every participant acting with common intention is liable.

title: Punishment for Gang Rape of Woman Under Eighteen Years of Age
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Where a woman under eighteen years of age is subjected to gang rape, every person acting in furtherance of the common intention is deemed guilty of the offence.
simpleExplanation: Gang rape of a minor girl is treated as an aggravated offence with extremely severe punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment for life (remainder of natural life) and fine, or death.
examples: A group of persons jointly commits sexual assault on a girl below eighteen years.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes, depending on the facts and gravity of the offence.

title: Disclosure of Identity of Victim of Certain Offences
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Prohibits publication or disclosure of the identity of victims of specified sexual offences except in circumstances permitted by law.
simpleExplanation: The identity of victims of sexual offences must be protected.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
examples: Publishing the name or photograph of a rape victim without authorization.

faqs.question: Why is victim identity protected?
faqs.answer: To preserve privacy, dignity and safety.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Woman with Intent to Outrage Her Modesty
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against a woman intending to outrage or knowing it likely to outrage her modesty commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Physical acts intended to insult, violate or outrage a woman's dignity are punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment of 1 to 5 years and fine.
examples: Inappropriately touching a woman against her will.

faqs.question: Is physical contact required?
faqs.answer: Yes, assault or criminal force must be involved.

title: Sexual Harassment
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Defines and punishes sexual harassment, including unwelcome physical contact, demands for sexual favours, showing pornography, or sexually coloured remarks.
simpleExplanation: Unwanted sexual behaviour directed at a woman constitutes sexual harassment.
punishment: Up to 3 years imprisonment, or fine, or both depending on the act.
examples: Demanding sexual favours at the workplace.
Making repeated sexually coloured remarks.

faqs.question: Can verbal conduct amount to sexual harassment?
faqs.answer: Yes, sexually coloured remarks are included.

title: Assault or Criminal Force to Woman with Intent to Disrobe
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against a woman with intent to disrobe or compel her to be naked commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Attempting to strip or forcibly remove a woman's clothing is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment of 3 to 7 years and fine.
examples: Forcibly removing a woman's clothes in public.

faqs.question: Is actual disrobing necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Intent to disrobe is sufficient.

title: Voyeurism
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever watches, captures or disseminates images of a woman engaging in a private act without her consent commits voyeurism.
simpleExplanation: Secretly watching or recording a woman in private situations is punishable.
punishment: First conviction: 1 to 3 years and fine; subsequent conviction: 3 to 7 years and fine.
examples: Secretly recording a woman in a changing room.

faqs.question: Does sharing such recordings increase liability?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dissemination is specifically covered.

title: Stalking
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever repeatedly follows, contacts, monitors or attempts to foster personal interaction with a woman despite her clear indication of disinterest commits stalking.
simpleExplanation: Persistent unwanted following, monitoring or contact with a woman is a criminal offence.
punishment: First conviction: up to 3 years and fine; subsequent conviction: up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Repeatedly tracking a woman's movements through social media.

faqs.question: Can online monitoring amount to stalking?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic monitoring is covered.

title: Word, Gesture or Act Intended to Insult Modesty of Woman
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever utters words, makes gestures or exhibits objects intending to insult the modesty of a woman commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Verbal, visual or behavioural acts intended to insult a woman's dignity are punishable.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Making obscene gestures directed at a woman.

faqs.question: Must there be physical contact?
faqs.answer: No. Words and gestures alone may constitute the offence.

title: Dowry Death
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Where a woman's death occurs under abnormal circumstances within seven years of marriage and she was subjected to dowry-related cruelty or harassment soon before her death, it is a dowry death.
simpleExplanation: Deaths linked to dowry harassment are treated as a distinct and serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment not less than 7 years, which may extend to imprisonment for life.
examples: A woman dies under suspicious circumstances after prolonged dowry harassment.

faqs.question: What is meant by 'soon before death'?
faqs.answer: A proximate connection between harassment and the death must exist.

title: Cohabitation Caused by Deceitful Means or False Promise of Marriage
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever induces a woman to cohabit or engage in a relationship by deceitful means, false promise of marriage or concealment of material facts commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Deceiving a woman into a relationship or cohabitation through false representations is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A person conceals an existing marriage and induces a woman into cohabitation.

faqs.question: Is every failed relationship covered?
faqs.answer: No. The deception must exist at the time of inducement.

oldIPCSection: Related to 493 IPC concepts; expanded in BNS

title: Marrying Again During Lifetime of Husband or Wife
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever, having a husband or wife living, marries again during the lifetime of such husband or wife commits an offence, subject to legally recognized exceptions.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot legally marry again while a spouse is still living unless an exception applies.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A married person enters into another marriage without legally dissolving the first marriage.

faqs.question: Does every second marriage amount to an offence?
faqs.answer: No. Certain legal exceptions may apply, such as a valid declaration of death.

title: Marriage Ceremony Fraudulently Gone Through Without Lawful Marriage
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently goes through a marriage ceremony knowing that no lawful marriage is thereby created commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to enter into a valid marriage while knowing it is not legally valid is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person conducts a sham marriage ceremony to deceive another person.

faqs.question: Must the person know the marriage is invalid?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of the invalidity is essential.

title: Enticing or Taking Away or Detaining with Criminal Intent a Married Woman
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Whoever entices, takes away, conceals or detains a married woman with criminal intent commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: The law punishes persons who interfere with a marital relationship through criminal conduct.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A person knowingly harbours a married woman for unlawful purposes.

faqs.question: Is every interaction with a married woman covered?
faqs.answer: No. Criminal intent must be proved.

title: Cruelty by Husband or Relatives of Husband
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: A husband or his relatives who subject a woman to cruelty, including conduct likely to drive her to suicide or cause grave injury, or harassment related to unlawful demands, commit an offence.
simpleExplanation: Cruelty and harassment by a husband or his relatives are criminal offences.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Persistent dowry harassment by a husband and in-laws.

faqs.question: What is meant by cruelty?
faqs.answer: Cruelty includes physical or mental harassment and unlawful demands.

title: Husband or Relative of Husband Subjecting Woman to Cruelty Resulting in Suicide or Serious Harm
chapter: Chapter V - Offences Against Woman and Child
officialText: Provides enhanced consequences where cruelty contributes to severe harm, suicide or related grave outcomes.
simpleExplanation: Serious consequences arising from domestic cruelty attract greater legal scrutiny and punishment.
punishment: Punishment as prescribed by the relevant provisions and circumstances.
examples: Cruel harassment leading a woman to attempt suicide.

faqs.question: Is proof of cruelty sufficient?
faqs.answer: The prosecution must establish the required connection between the cruelty and the resulting harm.

title: Kidnapping
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Defines kidnapping from India and kidnapping from lawful guardianship.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping involves taking a person out of India unlawfully or removing a minor or person of unsound mind from lawful guardianship.
punishment: Punishment prescribed in subsequent sections.
examples: Taking a minor away from lawful guardians without consent.

faqs.question: Can a minor consent to kidnapping?
faqs.answer: No. Consent of the lawful guardian is generally required.

title: Kidnapping or Maiming a Child for Purposes of Begging
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps, employs or maims a child for purposes of begging commits an aggravated offence.
simpleExplanation: Using children for begging or causing injury to facilitate begging is a serious crime.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine; life imprisonment where maiming is involved.
examples: A criminal gang kidnaps children and forces them to beg.

faqs.question: Why is maiming treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Because it causes permanent physical harm to the child.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting in Order to Murder or for Ransom
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Kidnapping or abducting a person with intent to murder, cause death, or obtain ransom constitutes a grave offence.
simpleExplanation: Kidnapping for murder or ransom is among the most serious kidnapping offences.
punishment: Death or imprisonment for life, and fine, in specified cases.
examples: Kidnapping a child and demanding ransom from the family.

faqs.question: Can the death penalty be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes, in appropriate ransom kidnapping cases.

title: Kidnapping or Abducting with Intent Secretly and Wrongfully to Confine Person
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps or abducts a person with intent to secretly and wrongfully confine that person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Abducting a person and keeping them hidden or unlawfully confined is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A person abducts another and keeps them hidden in a remote location.

faqs.question: Must confinement actually occur?
faqs.answer: The intent to secretly and wrongfully confine is essential.

title: Kidnapping, Abducting or Inducing Woman to Compel Marriage or Illicit Intercourse
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever kidnaps, abducts or induces a woman with intent that she may be compelled to marry or forced into illicit intercourse commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Forcing or coercing a woman into marriage or sexual exploitation is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Abducting a woman to compel her to marry against her will.

faqs.question: Is consent relevant?
faqs.answer: Yes. Coercion or unlawful inducement is a key element of the offence.

title: Procuration of Child
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever induces a child to go from any place or to do any act with intent that such child may be forced or seduced to illicit intercourse with another person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: It is a crime to lure or procure a child for sexual exploitation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A trafficker persuades a minor girl to leave home for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Is actual exploitation necessary?
faqs.answer: No. The intention to facilitate exploitation is sufficient.

title: Importation of Child from Foreign Country
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever imports into India a child from a foreign country with intent that such child may be forced or seduced to illicit intercourse commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Bringing a child into India for sexual exploitation is a serious crime.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Trafficking a minor from another country into India for prostitution.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to girls?
faqs.answer: The BNS uses gender-neutral language for child protection provisions.

title: Buying or Disposing of Any Person as a Slave
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever imports, exports, removes, buys, sells or disposes of any person as a slave commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Trading human beings as property is prohibited and punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Selling a person into forced labour.

faqs.question: Does slavery remain an offence under modern law?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any form of human ownership or trafficking is prohibited.

title: Habitual Dealing in Slaves
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever habitually imports, exports, removes, buys, sells or traffics persons as slaves commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Repeated involvement in slavery-related activities attracts criminal punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A criminal organization repeatedly engages in human trafficking.

faqs.question: What makes the offence habitual?
faqs.answer: Repeated or systematic involvement in trafficking or slavery activities.

title: Unlawful Compulsory Labour
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever unlawfully compels any person to labour against that person's will commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Forcing someone to work against their will is illegal.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A person is forced to work without freedom to leave.

faqs.question: Does this include bonded labour?
faqs.answer: Yes. Forced labour arrangements may fall within this provision.

title: Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed commits wrongful restraint.
simpleExplanation: Blocking a person's lawful movement amounts to wrongful restraint.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Preventing someone from leaving a public road.

faqs.question: Is temporary obstruction enough?
faqs.answer: Yes, if it unlawfully prevents movement.

title: Wrongful Confinement
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully restrains a person so as to prevent that person from proceeding beyond certain limits commits wrongful confinement.
simpleExplanation: Keeping a person confined within a restricted area against their will is wrongful confinement.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Locking a person inside a room against their will.

faqs.question: How is confinement different from restraint?
faqs.answer: Confinement restricts movement within fixed boundaries.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Three or More Days
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Provides enhanced punishment where wrongful confinement continues for three or more days.
simpleExplanation: Longer periods of unlawful confinement attract greater punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
examples: Keeping a person locked in a house for several days.

faqs.question: Why is punishment increased?
faqs.answer: Because prolonged confinement causes greater harm.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Ten or More Days
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Provides further enhanced punishment where wrongful confinement continues for ten or more days.
simpleExplanation: Extended unlawful confinement is treated more seriously.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: A kidnapping victim is unlawfully confined for two weeks.

faqs.question: Does duration affect punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Longer confinement attracts stricter penalties.

title: Wrongful Confinement of Person for Whose Liberation Writ Has Been Issued
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines a person knowing that a writ or order has been issued for that person's release commits an aggravated offence.
simpleExplanation: Ignoring a court order directing a person's release is a serious crime.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years in addition to any punishment for wrongful confinement.
examples: A person refuses to release someone despite a court order.

faqs.question: Why is this offence aggravated?
faqs.answer: Because it involves deliberate disobedience of judicial authority.

title: Wrongful Confinement in Secret
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person in such a manner as to prevent discovery of the confinement commits an aggravated form of wrongful confinement.
simpleExplanation: Secretly detaining a person so that others cannot discover the confinement is a more serious offence.
punishment: Additional imprisonment up to 2 years besides punishment for wrongful confinement.
examples: Keeping a person hidden in a secret room to avoid detection.

faqs.question: Why is secret confinement punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because it increases the victim's vulnerability and hinders rescue.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Extorting Property or Constraining to Illegal Act
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines a person for the purpose of extorting property, valuable security, or compelling an illegal act commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Confining someone to force payment, transfer property or perform an unlawful act is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Holding a person captive until money is paid.

faqs.question: Does the property actually need to be transferred?
faqs.answer: No. The intent to extort is sufficient.

title: Wrongful Confinement for Extorting Confession or Information
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines a person to compel a confession, information, or discovery of property commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot be unlawfully detained to force a confession or obtain information.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Detaining a suspect privately to force a confession.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to police officers?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to any person engaging in such conduct.

title: Trafficking of Person
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever recruits, transports, harbours, transfers or receives a person by force, coercion, fraud, abuse of power or inducement for exploitation commits trafficking.
simpleExplanation: Human trafficking includes recruiting or transporting people for exploitation through coercion, deception or abuse.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 7 years, extendable to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Recruiting workers through deception for forced labour.

faqs.question: Is consent a defence to trafficking?
faqs.answer: No, where the prohibited means of trafficking are used.

title: Trafficking of More Than One Person
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Provides enhanced punishment where trafficking involves more than one victim.
simpleExplanation: Trafficking multiple persons is punished more severely.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years, extendable to imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: A trafficking network transports several victims for exploitation.

faqs.question: Why is punishment enhanced?
faqs.answer: Because multiple victims increase the seriousness of the offence.

title: Exploitation of Trafficked Person
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever knowingly engages a trafficked person for sexual exploitation, slavery, servitude or forced labour commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Using or exploiting a person known to be trafficked is itself a crime.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 5 years and fine.
examples: Employing a trafficked person in forced labour conditions.

faqs.question: Must the offender know the victim was trafficked?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is an essential element.

title: Organized Crime
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Defines organized crime as continuing unlawful activity by members of a crime syndicate involving violence, intimidation, coercion or other unlawful means for obtaining material benefit.
simpleExplanation: Criminal gangs carrying out serious and repeated unlawful activities fall within organized crime.
punishment: Punishment varies according to consequences; may extend to death or life imprisonment in serious cases.
examples: A crime syndicate engaged in extortion, trafficking and contract killings.

faqs.question: What is a crime syndicate?
faqs.answer: A group of persons acting together in organized criminal activity.

title: Petty Organized Crime
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Defines and punishes petty organized criminal activities carried out by groups for unlawful gain.
simpleExplanation: Repeated minor crimes committed by organized groups are punishable under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A gang repeatedly involved in mobile phone thefts and pickpocketing.

faqs.question: How is petty organized crime different from organized crime?
faqs.answer: It generally involves less serious but recurring organized criminal activity.

title: Terrorist Act
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Defines terrorist acts involving threats to the unity, integrity, security or sovereignty of India through violence, intimidation or other prohibited means.
simpleExplanation: Acts intended to spread terror or threaten national security are treated as terrorist offences.
punishment: Death or imprisonment for life in serious cases; other punishments as specified.
examples: A coordinated bomb attack intended to spread terror among the public.

faqs.question: Why was this provision introduced in BNS?
faqs.answer: To incorporate specific terrorism-related offences within the criminal code.

title: Attempt to Commit Suicide to Compel Public Servant
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit suicide with intent to compel or deter a public servant from performing an official duty commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Using a suicide attempt as a means of coercing public authorities is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both, or community service.
examples: A person threatens self-harm to force a government official to take a particular action.

faqs.question: Is every suicide attempt punishable?
faqs.answer: No. This section specifically targets coercive attempts aimed at influencing public servants.

title: Wrongful Confinement in Secret
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever wrongfully confines any person in such a manner as to prevent discovery of the confinement commits an aggravated form of wrongful confinement.
simpleExplanation: Secretly detaining a person so that others cannot discover the confinement is a more serious offence.
punishment: Additional imprisonment up to 2 years besides punishment for wrongful confinement.
examples: Keeping a person hidden in a secret room to avoid detection.

faqs.question: Why is secret confinement punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because it increases the victim's vulnerability and hinders rescue.

examples: Holding a person captive until money is paid.

faqs.answer: No. The intent to extort is sufficient.

faqs.answer: No. It applies to any person engaging in such conduct.

title: Attempt to Commit Suicide to Compel Public Servant
chapter: Chapter VI - Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit suicide with intent to compel or deter a public servant from performing an official duty commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Using a suicide attempt as a means of coercing public authorities is punishable.

examples: A person threatens self-harm to force a government official to take a particular action.

faqs.question: Is every suicide attempt punishable?
faqs.answer: No. This section specifically targets coercive attempts aimed at influencing public servants.

title: Organised crime
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Any continuing unlawful activity including kidnapping, robbery, vehicle theft, extortion, land grabbing, contract killing, economic offence, cyber-crimes, trafficking of persons, drugs, weapons or illicit goods or services, human trafficking for prostitution or ransom, committed by a person or group acting as part of an organised crime syndicate for material or financial benefit constitutes organised crime.
simpleExplanation: This section targets criminal gangs and syndicates involved in repeated serious crimes for financial or material gain. It covers both direct participation and support activities connected to organised criminal networks.

punishment.ifDeathCaused: Death penalty or imprisonment for life and fine of not less than ₹10 lakh.
punishment.otherCases: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine not less than ₹5 lakh.
punishment.abetmentOrConspiracy: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine not less than ₹5 lakh.
punishment.membership: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine not less than ₹5 lakh.
punishment.harbouringOffender: Imprisonment not less than 3 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine not less than ₹5 lakh.
punishment.possessingCrimeProceeds: Imprisonment not less than 3 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine not less than ₹2 lakh.
punishment.unexplainedProperty: Imprisonment not less than 3 years and may extend to 10 years, and fine not less than ₹1 lakh.

examples: A criminal gang repeatedly conducts kidnappings and extortion for ransom.
A syndicate traffics drugs across states for profit.
A person knowingly stores money earned from organised crime.

faqs.question: What is an organised crime syndicate?
faqs.answer: A group of two or more persons acting together to carry out continuing unlawful activities.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished merely for being a member?
faqs.answer: Yes. Membership in an organised crime syndicate itself is punishable.

title: Petty organised crime
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
