officialText: A member of a group or gang who commits theft, snatching, cheating, unauthorised ticket selling, unauthorised betting, gambling, selling examination question papers, or similar criminal acts commits petty organised crime.
simpleExplanation: This section deals with small-scale organised criminal activities carried out by gangs or groups, even if the crimes are less serious than large organised crime operations.
punishment: Imprisonment for not less than 1 year and up to 7 years, and fine.
examples: A gang repeatedly pickpockets passengers at railway stations.
A group sells leaked public examination question papers.
A gang conducts illegal betting operations.

faqs.question: Does shoplifting fall under petty organised crime?
faqs.answer: Yes, if committed by a gang or organised group.

faqs.question: Is card-skimming included?
faqs.answer: Yes. Theft through card skimming is specifically included.

title: Terrorist act
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Any act intended to threaten the unity, integrity, sovereignty, security, or economic security of India, or intended to strike terror among people through violence, explosives, weapons, hazardous substances, kidnapping, destruction of property, disruption of essential services, or similar means constitutes a terrorist act.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalizes acts of terrorism, including violent attacks, bombings, hostage-taking, recruitment, training, financing, and support activities intended to threaten India or create terror among people.

punishment.ifDeathCaused: Death penalty or imprisonment for life and fine.
punishment.otherCases: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.conspiracyOrAbetment: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.trainingOrRecruitment: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.membership: Imprisonment up to life imprisonment and fine.
punishment.harbouringTerrorist: Imprisonment not less than 3 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.possessingTerrorProperty: Imprisonment up to life imprisonment and fine.

examples: Planting bombs to create fear among the public.
Kidnapping individuals to force government action.
Recruiting people into a terrorist organisation.

faqs.question: Can conspiracy alone be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Conspiracy, preparation, recruitment, and facilitation are punishable.

faqs.question: Who decides whether a case is registered under this section?
faqs.answer: An officer not below the rank of Superintendent of Police.

title: Hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.
simpleExplanation: Any act that causes physical pain, illness, or bodily weakness to another person amounts to hurt.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is provided under subsequent sections.
examples: Punching someone and causing pain.
Infecting a person with a disease intentionally.

faqs.question: Is physical injury necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Pain, disease, or infirmity is sufficient.

title: Hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes bodily pain, disease or infirmity to any person is said to cause hurt.
simpleExplanation: Any act that causes physical pain, illness, or bodily weakness to another person amounts to hurt.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is provided under subsequent sections.
examples: Punching someone and causing pain.
Infecting a person with a disease intentionally.

faqs.question: Is physical injury necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Pain, disease, or infirmity is sufficient.

title: Voluntarily causing hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever intentionally or knowingly causes hurt to another person voluntarily causes hurt.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when they intentionally or knowingly inflict bodily pain, disease, or infirmity on another person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: Intentionally slapping someone causing pain.
Deliberately striking a person during an argument.

faqs.question: Does intention matter?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be intentional or done with knowledge that hurt is likely.

title: Grievous hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: The following kinds of hurt only are designated as grievous hurt: (a) emasculation; (b) permanent privation of sight of either eye; (c) permanent privation of hearing of either ear; (d) privation of any member or joint; (e) destruction or permanent impairing of powers of any member or joint; (f) permanent disfiguration of the head or face; (g) fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth; (h) any hurt which endangers life or causes severe bodily pain for fifteen days or renders a person unable to follow ordinary pursuits.
simpleExplanation: This section defines the serious categories of injuries that the law treats as grievous hurt. These injuries are more severe than ordinary hurt and attract higher punishment.
punishment: Definition section only. Punishment is prescribed under Section 117 and related sections.
examples: Breaking a person's arm.
Causing permanent blindness in one eye.
Disfiguring a person's face with a weapon.

faqs.question: Is a fractured tooth considered grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fracture or dislocation of a bone or tooth is grievous hurt.

faqs.question: Is permanent facial disfigurement grievous hurt?
faqs.answer: Yes. Permanent disfiguration of the head or face is specifically included.

title: Voluntarily causing grievous hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt, intending or knowing that grievous hurt is likely to be caused, and grievous hurt is actually caused, commits the offence of voluntarily causing grievous hurt.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when they intentionally cause or knowingly risk causing serious injuries that qualify as grievous hurt.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.permanentDisabilityOrVegetativeState: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years and may extend to imprisonment for life.
punishment.hateCrimeByGroup: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

examples: Breaking another person's leg during an assault.
Attacking someone and causing permanent facial disfigurement.
A mob of five or more persons causing grievous hurt based on caste or religion.

faqs.question: Can a person be guilty if a different type of grievous hurt results?
faqs.answer: Yes. If grievous hurt was intended or known to be likely, liability remains even if a different type of grievous hurt occurs.

faqs.question: Is permanent disability treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Yes. Causing permanent disability or persistent vegetative state attracts enhanced punishment.

title: Voluntarily causing hurt or grievous hurt by dangerous weapons or means
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt or grievous hurt by dangerous weapons, fire, poison, corrosive substances, explosives, harmful substances, or animals commits an aggravated form of the offence.
simpleExplanation: This section provides enhanced punishment when hurt or grievous hurt is caused using dangerous methods or weapons that are capable of causing serious injury or death.

punishment.hurt: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine up to ₹20,000, or both.
punishment.grievousHurt: Life imprisonment or imprisonment from 1 year up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Stabbing a person with a knife.
Throwing acid or corrosive chemicals causing injury.
Poisoning a person's food.

faqs.question: Are knives covered under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. Instruments for shooting, stabbing or cutting are specifically covered.

faqs.question: Can an animal be treated as a weapon?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hurt caused through the use of an animal is included.

title: Voluntarily causing hurt or grievous hurt to extort property, or to constrain to an illegal act
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt or grievous hurt to extort property or valuable security, or to compel a person to perform an illegal act or facilitate an offence, commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes the use of violence to force someone to hand over property, money, valuables, or participate in illegal activities.

punishment.hurt: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.grievousHurt: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Beating a person until they transfer money.
Threatening and injuring someone to force them to sign property documents.
Assaulting a victim to compel participation in a crime.

faqs.question: Does this section apply only to money?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to any property or valuable security.

faqs.question: Is forcing a person to commit an illegal act covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Compelling a person to do anything illegal is covered.

title: Voluntarily causing hurt or grievous hurt to extort confession, or to compel restoration of property
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt or grievous hurt to obtain a confession, information relating to an offence, restoration of property, satisfaction of a claim, or information leading to recovery of property commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalises torture or violence used to extract confessions, obtain information, recover property, or enforce claims.

punishment.hurt: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.grievousHurt: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: A police officer beats a suspect to obtain a confession.
A person tortures another to discover where stolen goods are hidden.
A creditor assaults a debtor to force repayment.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to police officers?
faqs.answer: Yes. The illustrations specifically include police officers using torture to obtain confessions.

faqs.question: Is forcing someone to reveal the location of property covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Obtaining information leading to recovery of property is specifically covered.

title: Voluntarily causing hurt or grievous hurt to deter public servant from duty
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes hurt or grievous hurt to a public servant while discharging official duties, or to prevent or deter such duties, commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section protects public servants from violence while performing their lawful duties.

punishment.hurt: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.grievousHurt: Imprisonment not less than 1 year and up to 10 years, and fine.

examples: Assaulting a police officer during an arrest.
Injuring a revenue officer to stop an inspection.

faqs.question: Does this apply only while the public servant is on duty?
faqs.answer: It also applies when violence is used because of actions lawfully performed in official duty.

title: Voluntarily causing hurt or grievous hurt on grave and sudden provocation
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Provides reduced punishment where hurt or grievous hurt is caused due to grave and sudden provocation by the victim.
simpleExplanation: The law recognizes that a person may temporarily lose self-control due to serious and sudden provocation and therefore provides a lesser punishment.

punishment.hurt: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
punishment.grievousHurt: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.

examples: A person immediately reacts to a grave insult and causes hurt.
A sudden provocation leads to an assault causing grievous hurt.

faqs.question: Can this defence apply if an innocent person is injured?
faqs.answer: No. The offender must not intend or know that harm is likely to be caused to anyone other than the person giving the provocation.

title: Causing hurt by means of poison, etc., with intent to commit an offence
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever administers poison, intoxicating substances, stupefying drugs, or other harmful substances with intent to cause hurt or facilitate an offence commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes poisoning or drugging another person to cause harm or help commit a crime.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Mixing sleeping pills into someone's drink to commit theft.
Poisoning food to harm another person.

faqs.question: Must actual hurt occur?
faqs.answer: The administration of the substance with the required intent is sufficient for liability.

title: Voluntarily causing grievous hurt by use of acid, etc.
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever causes permanent or partial damage, deformity, burns, maiming, disfigurement, disability, grievous hurt, or a permanent vegetative state by throwing or administering acid commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section specifically deals with acid attacks and attempted acid attacks, imposing severe punishment due to the devastating impact on victims.

punishment.acidAttack: Imprisonment not less than 10 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.attempt: Imprisonment not less than 5 years and may extend to 7 years, and fine.

examples: Throwing acid on a person's face causing disfigurement.
Attempting to throw acid but being stopped before contact.

faqs.question: Must the injury be permanent?
faqs.answer: No. Permanent damage is not required to be irreversible.

faqs.question: Who receives the fine?
faqs.answer: The fine should help meet medical expenses and be paid to the victim.

title: Act endangering life or personal safety of others
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Offences Affecting the Human Body
officialText: Whoever rashly or negligently acts in a manner that endangers human life or personal safety commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes reckless or negligent conduct that creates danger to others.

punishment.endangermentOnly: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹2,500, or both.
punishment.hurtCaused: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
punishment.grievousHurtCaused: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.

examples: Carelessly handling hazardous equipment.
Reckless conduct causing serious injury.

faqs.question: Is intention required?
faqs.answer: No. Rashness or negligence is sufficient.

title: Wrongful restraint
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
officialText: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any person so as to prevent that person from proceeding in any direction in which that person has a right to proceed is said to wrongfully restrain that person.
simpleExplanation: Preventing someone from moving where they have a legal right to go amounts to wrongful restraint.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Blocking a road to prevent a person from passing.
Physically standing in a doorway to stop someone leaving.

faqs.question: Is blocking a private path always an offence?
faqs.answer: No. A person acting in good faith believing they have a lawful right may be protected by the exception.

title: Wrongful confinement
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Wrongful Restraint and Wrongful Confinement
officialText: Whoever wrongfully restrains a person so as to prevent movement beyond certain limits is said to wrongfully confine that person.
simpleExplanation: Wrongful confinement occurs when a person is unlawfully kept within a restricted area and prevented from leaving.

punishment.basic: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
punishment.threeDaysOrMore: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
punishment.tenDaysOrMore: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine not less than ₹10,000.
punishment.secretConfinement: Additional imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

examples: Locking a person in a room.
Preventing someone from leaving a building through armed guards.

faqs.question: Does duration affect punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Longer periods of confinement attract more severe punishment.

title: Force
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: A person uses force when he causes motion, change of motion, or cessation of motion to another person or to an object affecting that person's body or senses.
simpleExplanation: This section legally defines what amounts to the use of force.
punishment: Definition section only.
examples: Pushing a person.
Throwing an object that strikes another person.

faqs.question: Can force be applied through an animal?
faqs.answer: Yes. Force may be caused by inducing an animal to move.

title: Criminal force
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever intentionally uses force without consent in order to commit an offence or to cause injury, fear, or annoyance commits criminal force.
simpleExplanation: Criminal force occurs when force is intentionally used against another person without consent for unlawful purposes.
punishment: Punishment provided under subsequent specific sections involving criminal force.
examples: Pushing a person to intimidate them.
Pulling a woman's veil without consent.
Inciting a dog to attack another person.

faqs.question: Is consent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. Criminal force requires absence of consent.

title: Assault
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever makes any gesture or preparation intending or knowing it to be likely that another person will apprehend imminent use of criminal force commits assault.
simpleExplanation: Assault occurs when a person creates a reasonable fear that criminal force is about to be used, even if no physical contact occurs.
punishment: Definition section. Punishment is provided under later assault-related provisions.
examples: Raising a fist threateningly at another person.
Preparing to unleash a dangerous dog to frighten someone.
Brandishing a stick in a threatening manner.

faqs.question: Can words alone amount to assault?
faqs.answer: No. Mere words are not assault, though they may give threatening meaning to gestures.

title: Punishment for assault or criminal force otherwise than on grave provocation
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force to any person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation given by that person shall be punished under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes a person who assaults or uses criminal force against another person without grave and sudden provocation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A slaps another person during an argument without any grave provocation.
A pushes a person intentionally to intimidate him.

faqs.question: Does every provocation reduce punishment?
faqs.answer: No. Only grave and sudden provocation may mitigate punishment.

title: Assault or criminal force to deter public servant from discharge of duty
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force against a public servant while discharging official duties or to prevent such discharge commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: Protects public servants from assault or criminal force while performing official duties.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Assaulting a police officer during an arrest.
Using force against a government inspector conducting an official inspection.

faqs.question: Does the section apply after the official act is completed?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the assault is in consequence of a lawful act performed by the public servant.

title: Assault or criminal force with intent to dishonour person otherwise than on grave provocation
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force intending to dishonour a person otherwise than on grave and sudden provocation commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Punishes assaults committed for the purpose of insulting or humiliating another person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Forcibly removing a person's head covering to humiliate them.
Spitting on a person with intent to insult.

faqs.question: Must there be intent to dishonour?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence specifically requires intent to dishonour.

title: Assault or criminal force in attempt to commit theft of property carried by a person
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force while attempting to commit theft of property being worn or carried by a person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Punishes force used during an attempt to steal property from a person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Attempting to snatch a handbag by pushing the victim.
Trying to pull a gold chain from someone's neck.

faqs.question: Is actual theft required?
faqs.answer: No. An attempt accompanied by assault or criminal force is sufficient.

title: Assault or criminal force in attempt to wrongfully confine a person
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force in attempting wrongfully to confine another person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Punishes force used to unlawfully restrain or confine another person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Using force to drag a person into a locked room.
Physically restraining someone to prevent escape.

faqs.question: Is actual confinement necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Attempting wrongful confinement using force is sufficient.

title: Assault or criminal force on grave provocation
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Criminal Force and Assault
officialText: Whoever assaults or uses criminal force on grave and sudden provocation given by that person commits an offence under this section.
simpleExplanation: Provides reduced punishment when assault occurs because of grave and sudden provocation.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: A person immediately reacts to grave and sudden provocation by pushing another.

faqs.question: What kind of provocation is required?
faqs.answer: The provocation must be both grave and sudden.

title: Kidnapping
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Defines kidnapping from India and kidnapping from lawful guardianship and prescribes punishment.
simpleExplanation: A person commits kidnapping either by taking someone out of India without lawful consent or by taking a child or person of unsound mind away from their lawful guardian without consent.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Taking a minor away from parents without consent.
Taking a person outside India without legal consent.

faqs.question: Who is a lawful guardian?
faqs.answer: Any person lawfully entrusted with the care or custody of the child or person of unsound mind.

title: Abduction
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Whoever by force compels, or by deceitful means induces, any person to go from any place is said to abduct that person.
simpleExplanation: Abduction occurs when a person is forced or deceitfully persuaded to leave a place.
punishment: Definition section. Punishment depends on the purpose of the abduction under subsequent sections.
examples: Forcing a person into a vehicle.
Deceiving someone into travelling for unlawful purposes.

faqs.question: Is force always required?
faqs.answer: No. Deceitful inducement can also amount to abduction.

title: Kidnapping or maiming a child for purposes of begging
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Punishes kidnapping, obtaining custody, or maiming a child for the purpose of begging.
simpleExplanation: This section protects children from being kidnapped, exploited, or mutilated for begging activities.

punishment.kidnappingForBegging: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.maimingForBegging: Imprisonment not less than 20 years and may extend to life imprisonment for remainder of natural life, and fine.

examples: Kidnapping a child and forcing them to beg.
Maiming a child to increase sympathy and collect alms.

faqs.question: What is meant by begging?
faqs.answer: It includes soliciting alms, using a child as an exhibit, fortune-telling, performing tricks, and similar activities.

title: Kidnapping or abduction for murder, ransom, secret confinement, slavery or grievous hurt
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Punishes kidnapping or abduction for murder, ransom, secret confinement, slavery, grievous hurt, or exploitation.
simpleExplanation: This section covers the most serious forms of kidnapping and abduction, including ransom kidnappings and kidnappings intended for murder or slavery.

punishment.forMurder: Life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.forRansom: Death or imprisonment for life and fine.
punishment.forSecretConfinement: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.forGrievousHurtOrSlavery: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Kidnapping a person and demanding ransom.
Abducting a person to keep them secretly confined.
Kidnapping a person for slavery or exploitation.

faqs.question: Can ransom kidnapping attract the death penalty?
faqs.answer: Yes. Kidnapping for ransom under subsection (2) may be punishable with death.

title: Importation of girl or boy from foreign country
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Whoever imports into India from any country outside India any girl under twenty-one years of age or any boy under eighteen years of age with intent that such girl or boy may be forced or seduced to illicit intercourse shall be punished under this section.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes bringing minors into India for sexual exploitation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: A trafficker brings a 17-year-old boy into India for sexual exploitation.
A person imports a minor girl from another country intending prostitution.

faqs.question: Does actual exploitation need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. Intention or knowledge of likely exploitation is sufficient.

title: Wrongfully concealing or keeping in confinement kidnapped or abducted person
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Whoever knowingly conceals or confines a kidnapped or abducted person shall be punished as if he himself committed the kidnapping or abduction.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly hides or detains a kidnapped victim is treated like the kidnapper.
punishment: Same punishment as the kidnapping or abduction offence involved.
examples: Knowingly hiding a kidnapped child in a house.
Keeping an abducted person confined for ransom.

faqs.question: Must the person know the victim was kidnapped?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge is an essential element.

title: Trafficking of person
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Whoever recruits, transports, harbours, transfers or receives a person for exploitation through threats, force, coercion, abduction, fraud, deception, abuse of power or inducement commits trafficking.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalizes human trafficking for exploitation including sexual exploitation, slavery, forced labour, beggary and organ removal.

punishment.general: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 7 years and up to 10 years, and fine.
punishment.multipleVictims: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.childVictim: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.multipleChildren: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 14 years and may extend to life imprisonment, and fine.
punishment.repeatChildTrafficking: Life imprisonment for remainder of natural life and fine.
punishment.publicServantInvolved: Life imprisonment for remainder of natural life and fine.

examples: Transporting persons through deception for forced labour.
Recruiting children for commercial sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Does victim consent matter?
faqs.answer: No. Consent of the victim is immaterial.

title: Exploitation of a trafficked person
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Punishes persons who knowingly engage trafficked persons for sexual exploitation.
simpleExplanation: This section targets individuals who exploit trafficked victims, especially for sexual purposes.

punishment.childVictim: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 5 years and up to 10 years, and fine.
punishment.adultVictim: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 3 years and up to 7 years, and fine.

examples: Knowingly employing a trafficked child in prostitution.
Using a trafficked adult for sexual exploitation.

faqs.question: Must the offender know the person was trafficked?
faqs.answer: Knowledge or reason to believe is sufficient.

title: Habitual dealing in slaves
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Whoever habitually imports, exports, removes, buys, sells, traffics or deals in slaves commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes persons involved in the repeated trade or trafficking of slaves.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Running an illegal human slave trade network.
Repeated buying and selling of persons as slaves.

faqs.question: Is one transaction enough?
faqs.answer: The section specifically targets habitual dealing.

title: Unlawful compulsory labour
chapter: Chapter VI - Of Kidnapping, Abduction, Slavery and Forced Labour
officialText: Whoever unlawfully compels any person to labour against the will of that person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits forcing a person to work against their will.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Forcing workers to continue labour through threats.
Compelling bonded labour without consent.

faqs.question: Does the labour have to be unpaid?
faqs.answer: No. Any unlawful compulsion to work may attract liability.

title: Waging war against Government of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever wages war against the Government of India, attempts to wage war, or abets such war commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes armed rebellion or warfare against the Government of India.
punishment: Death or imprisonment for life, and fine.
examples: Joining an armed insurrection against the Government of India.

faqs.question: Is attempt punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting or abetting war is punishable.

title: Conspiracy to commit offences punishable by section 147
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever conspires to wage war against the Government of India or to overawe the Government by criminal force commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes conspiracies relating to war against the State.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Planning an armed rebellion against the Government.

faqs.question: Is an overt act necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Mere conspiracy is sufficient.

title: Collecting arms, etc., with intention of waging war
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever collects men, arms, ammunition or otherwise prepares to wage war against the Government of India commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes preparations for waging war against the State.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Stockpiling weapons for an armed rebellion.

faqs.question: Must war actually begin?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation itself is punishable.

title: Concealing design to wage war
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever conceals a design to wage war against the Government of India with intent to facilitate such war commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes persons who knowingly hide plans to wage war against the Government.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Knowingly hiding information about a planned armed uprising.

faqs.question: Must the person actively participate in the war?
faqs.answer: No. Concealing the design itself is punishable.

title: Assaulting President, Governor, etc., with intent to compel or restrain exercise of lawful power
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever assaults, wrongfully restrains, or overawes the President of India or a Governor with intent to compel or restrain the exercise of lawful powers commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section protects the constitutional authority of the President and Governors from coercion, intimidation, or unlawful restraint.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Threatening a Governor to force a particular constitutional decision.
Wrongfully restraining the President to prevent the exercise of official powers.

faqs.question: Does an attempt attract punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempting to wrongfully restrain or overawe is also punishable.

title: Act endangering sovereignty, unity and integrity of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever purposely or knowingly excites or attempts to excite secession, armed rebellion, subversive activities, separatist activities, or endangers the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes activities aimed at breaking up India, promoting armed rebellion, or threatening national unity and sovereignty.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 7 years, and fine.
examples: Organising an armed separatist movement.
Funding activities intended to promote secession.

faqs.question: Is criticism of the Government punishable?
faqs.answer: No. Lawful criticism seeking policy change without promoting secession, rebellion, or separatism is not an offence.

title: Waging war against Government of any foreign State at peace with Government of India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever wages war against a foreign State that is at peace with India, attempts such war, or abets such war commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section protects India's peaceful relations with friendly foreign countries.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine.
examples: Organising armed attacks against a friendly foreign nation.

faqs.question: Can abetment alone be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Abetment of such war is punishable.

title: Committing depredation on territories of foreign State at peace with India
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever commits or prepares to commit depredation on the territory of a foreign State at peace with India commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes looting, plundering, or hostile acts against friendly foreign territories.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, and forfeiture of property used or acquired.
examples: Organising armed raids into a friendly foreign country's territory.

faqs.question: Can preparation alone be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preparation to commit depredation is punishable.

title: Receiving property taken by war or depredation
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever knowingly receives property obtained through offences under Sections 153 or 154 commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes dealing in property obtained through unlawful war or depredation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, fine, and forfeiture of property.
examples: Purchasing goods known to have been looted during a hostile raid.

faqs.question: Is knowledge required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know the property was unlawfully obtained.

title: Public servant voluntarily allowing State prisoner or prisoner of war to escape
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: A public servant who voluntarily allows a State prisoner or prisoner of war to escape commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes deliberate facilitation of escape by officials responsible for custody.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: A jail officer intentionally allows a prisoner of war to escape.

faqs.question: Does negligence apply here?
faqs.answer: No. This section applies to voluntary acts. Negligence is covered separately.

title: Public servant negligently suffering State prisoner or prisoner of war to escape
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: A public servant who negligently allows a State prisoner or prisoner of war to escape commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes careless conduct resulting in the escape of high-security prisoners.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Failing to secure prison facilities resulting in escape.

faqs.question: What distinguishes this from Section 156?
faqs.answer: Section 157 covers negligence, while Section 156 covers intentional conduct.

title: Aiding escape of State prisoner or prisoner of war
chapter: Chapter VII - Of Offences Against the State
officialText: Whoever assists, rescues, harbours, conceals, or helps a State prisoner or prisoner of war escape lawful custody commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes all forms of assistance given to escaped State prisoners or prisoners of war.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Providing shelter to an escaped prisoner of war.
Helping a State prisoner evade recapture.

faqs.question: Does harbouring an escaped prisoner count?
faqs.answer: Yes. Harbouring or concealing is specifically punishable.

title: Abetting mutiny or attempting to seduce military personnel from duty
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets mutiny or attempts to seduce military personnel from allegiance or duty commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section protects discipline and loyalty within the armed forces.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Encouraging soldiers to disobey lawful military orders.

faqs.question: Does actual mutiny need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. Attempting to seduce personnel from duty is sufficient.

title: Abetment of mutiny if mutiny is committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets mutiny and such mutiny is committed as a consequence commits an aggravated offence.
simpleExplanation: This section imposes enhanced punishment when abetment actually results in mutiny.
punishment: Death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Inciting soldiers to revolt, resulting in an actual mutiny.

faqs.question: Why is punishment more severe than Section 159?
faqs.answer: Because the mutiny was actually carried out as a result of the abetment.

title: Abetment of assault by soldier, sailor or airman on superior officer
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets an assault by an officer, soldier, sailor or airman upon a superior officer in the execution of his office commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes persons who encourage or assist military personnel to assault their superior officers while performing official duties.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Encouraging a soldier to attack a commanding officer during duty.

faqs.question: Is actual assault required?
faqs.answer: No. Mere abetment itself is punishable under this section.

title: Abetment of such assault if assault committed
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets an assault by military personnel on a superior officer and such assault is committed in consequence of that abetment commits an aggravated offence.
simpleExplanation: This section imposes a higher punishment where the encouraged assault actually takes place.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Persuading a soldier to attack a superior officer and the attack occurs.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher than Section 161?
faqs.answer: Because the assault was actually committed.

title: Abetment of desertion of soldier, sailor or airman
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets the desertion of any officer, soldier, sailor or airman commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes those who encourage military personnel to abandon service.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Helping a soldier plan and execute desertion.

faqs.question: Is actual desertion necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Abetment itself is punishable.

title: Harbouring deserter
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever knowingly harbours a deserter from the Army, Navy or Air Force commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes those who shelter military deserters.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Providing accommodation to a soldier known to have deserted.

faqs.question: Does the spouse of the deserter commit an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The spouse is specifically exempted.

title: Deserter concealed on board merchant vessel through negligence of master
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: The master or person in charge of a merchant vessel may be penalized if a military deserter is concealed on board due to negligence.
simpleExplanation: Ship masters must exercise reasonable care to prevent deserters from hiding aboard their vessels.
punishment: Penalty up to ₹3,000.
examples: A deserter hides on a merchant ship due to lack of proper inspection.

faqs.question: Is actual knowledge required?
faqs.answer: No. Negligence resulting in concealment is sufficient.

title: Abetment of act of insubordination by soldier, sailor or airman
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever abets an act of insubordination by military personnel and such act occurs commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes those who encourage military personnel to disobey lawful authority.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Inciting soldiers to refuse lawful orders.

faqs.question: Must the insubordination actually occur?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be committed in consequence of the abetment.

title: Persons subject to Army Act, Navy Act and Air Force Act
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Persons subject to the Army Act, Air Force Act or Navy Act are governed by those laws and not punishable under this Chapter for the same offences.
simpleExplanation: This section clarifies the jurisdiction of military laws over military personnel.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A serving soldier is tried under the Army Act rather than this Chapter.

faqs.question: Why does this section exist?
faqs.answer: To avoid overlapping punishments under military and civilian law.

title: Wearing garb or carrying token used by soldier, sailor or airman
chapter: Chapter VIII - Of Offences Relating to the Army, Navy and Air Force
officialText: Whoever wears military uniform or carries military insignia with intent to make others believe he is a member of the armed forces commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents impersonation of military personnel.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹2,000, or both.
examples: A civilian wears military uniform to obtain privileges.

faqs.question: Is wearing military-style clothing always illegal?
faqs.answer: No. The offence requires intent to deceive others into believing the person is military personnel.

title: Candidate and electoral right defined
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Defines candidate and electoral right for the purposes of election offences.
simpleExplanation: This section provides key definitions used in election-related offences.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A nominated election contestant is a candidate under this Chapter.

faqs.question: What is an electoral right?
faqs.answer: It includes the right to contest, withdraw, vote, or refrain from voting.

title: Bribery
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Giving or accepting gratification to influence the exercise of an electoral right constitutes bribery.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits offering or accepting benefits in exchange for voting behavior or electoral influence.
punishment: Punishment prescribed under election offence provisions.
examples: Paying voters to vote for a particular candidate.
Accepting money in exchange for influencing voters.

faqs.question: Is a political promise considered bribery?
faqs.answer: No. A declaration of public policy or promise of public action is not bribery.

title: Undue Influence at Elections
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever voluntarily interferes or attempts to interfere with the free exercise of any electoral right commits the offence of undue influence at an election. Threatening a candidate or voter with injury, or inducing fear of divine displeasure or spiritual censure, is deemed to be interference with electoral rights.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits any attempt to pressure, threaten, intimidate, or improperly influence voters or candidates in exercising their voting rights freely.
punishment: Punishment provided under Section 174 – imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening a voter with harm if they vote for a particular candidate.
Telling voters they will suffer divine punishment if they support a specific candidate.

faqs.question: Does campaigning for a political policy amount to undue influence?
faqs.answer: No. A declaration of public policy, a promise of public action, or the lawful exercise of a legal right without intent to interfere with electoral rights is not undue influence.

title: Personation at Elections
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever applies for a voting paper in the name of another person, whether living or dead, in a fictitious name, or votes more than once in the same election, commits the offence of personation at an election.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes impersonating another voter, using a fake identity to vote, or attempting to vote multiple times in the same election.
punishment: Punishment provided under Section 174 – imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.
examples: Casting a vote using the identity of a deceased voter.
Voting twice in the same election using different identities.

faqs.question: Does this apply to authorized proxy voting?
faqs.answer: No. Authorized proxy voting under applicable law is exempt from this section.

title: Punishment for Bribery
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever commits the offence of bribery shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year, or with fine, or with both. Bribery by treating shall be punished with fine only.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for election bribery, including giving money, gifts, food, drinks, or entertainment to influence voting behavior.
punishment: Imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both. Bribery by treating is punishable with fine only.
examples: Paying voters to support a candidate.
Providing free meals to voters in exchange for votes.

faqs.question: What is 'treating'?
faqs.answer: Treating is a form of bribery where the gratification consists of food, drink, entertainment, or provision.

title: Punishment for Undue Influence or Personation at an Election
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever commits the offence of undue influence or personation at an election shall be punished with imprisonment of either description for a term which may extend to one year or with fine, or with both.
simpleExplanation: This section provides the punishment for election offences involving voter intimidation, coercion, or impersonation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to one year, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening voters to influence election results.
Impersonating another voter to cast a ballot.

faqs.question: Which offences are covered by this section?
faqs.answer: Undue influence under Section 171 and personation under Section 172.

title: False Statement in Connection with an Election
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever with intent to affect the result of an election makes or publishes any statement of fact which is false and which he knows or believes to be false, relating to the personal character or conduct of a candidate, shall be punished with fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits spreading knowingly false statements about a candidate's personal character or conduct to influence election results.
punishment: Fine.
examples: Publishing a fabricated criminal allegation against a candidate.
Spreading false rumors about a candidate's personal conduct before voting.

faqs.question: Does this apply to opinions?
faqs.answer: No. The section specifically applies to false statements presented as facts.

title: Illegal Payments in Connection with an Election
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever without the general or special authority in writing of a candidate incurs or authorises expenses for public meetings, advertisements, publications, or other activities for promoting the election of such candidate shall be punished with fine.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits spending money on behalf of a candidate's election campaign without written authorization from the candidate.
punishment: Fine up to ten thousand rupees.
examples: Paying for campaign advertisements without the candidate's approval.
Organizing and funding election rallies without written authority.

faqs.question: Can unauthorized expenses be later approved?
faqs.answer: Yes. Expenses not exceeding ten rupees may be treated as authorized if written approval is obtained from the candidate within ten days.

title: Failure to Keep Election Accounts
chapter: Chapter IX - Of Offences Relating to Elections
officialText: Whoever being required by law to keep accounts of expenses incurred at or in connection with an election fails to keep such accounts shall be punished with fine.
simpleExplanation: This section requires persons legally obligated to maintain election expense records to do so accurately and completely.
punishment: Fine up to five thousand rupees.
examples: A candidate failing to maintain records of campaign expenditures.
Not keeping legally required election expense accounts.

faqs.question: Why are election accounts important?
faqs.answer: They promote transparency and accountability in election spending.

title: Counterfeiting Coin, Government Stamps, Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever counterfeits, or knowingly performs any part of the process of counterfeiting, any coin, Government revenue stamp, currency-note, or bank-note shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: This section criminalizes making or helping to make fake coins, stamps, currency notes, or bank notes.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years, and fine.
examples: Printing fake currency notes for circulation.
Altering genuine coins to make them resemble coins of higher value.

faqs.question: Does altering genuine currency amount to counterfeiting?
faqs.answer: Yes, if done with the intention of deception or to make it appear as a different denomination or type.

title: Using as Genuine, Forged or Counterfeit Coin, Government Stamp, Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever imports, exports, sells, buys, receives, traffics in, or uses as genuine any forged or counterfeit coin, stamp, currency-note, or bank-note knowing it to be forged or counterfeit shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes dealing in or using counterfeit money, stamps, or coins while knowing they are fake.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years, and fine.
examples: Selling counterfeit currency to another person.
Knowingly using fake notes in commercial transactions.

faqs.question: Is knowledge that the item is counterfeit necessary?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know or have reason to believe that it is forged or counterfeit.

title: Possession of Forged or Counterfeit Coin, Government Stamp, Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever possesses any forged or counterfeit coin, stamp, currency-note, or bank-note knowing or having reason to believe it to be forged or counterfeit and intending to use it as genuine shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes possession of counterfeit currency, stamps, or coins when the person knows they are fake and intends to use them as genuine.
punishment: Imprisonment up to seven years, or fine, or both.
examples: Keeping counterfeit currency for future use in transactions.
Possessing fake stamps intending to sell or use them.

faqs.question: Is possession from a lawful source an offence?
faqs.answer: No. If the person proves the counterfeit item came from a lawful source, it does not constitute an offence under this section.

title: Making or Possessing Instruments or Materials for Forging or Counterfeiting Coin, Government Stamp, Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, mends, buys, sells, possesses, or deals with any machinery, die, instrument, or material intended for forging or counterfeiting any coin, Government revenue stamp, currency-note, or bank-note commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes possession, manufacture, sale, or repair of tools and materials used for producing counterfeit currency, coins, or Government stamps.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or imprisonment up to ten years, and fine.
examples: Manufacturing printing plates for fake currency notes.
Possessing specialized dies intended for producing counterfeit coins.

faqs.question: Is actual counterfeiting required for an offence?
faqs.answer: No. Possessing or dealing with equipment intended for counterfeiting is sufficient.

title: Making or Using Documents Resembling Currency-Notes or Bank-Notes
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, uses, distributes, or delivers any document resembling or purporting to be a currency-note or bank-note in a manner likely to deceive commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits creating or circulating imitation documents that resemble genuine currency or bank notes and may mislead people.
punishment: Fine up to three hundred rupees. Failure to disclose the printer or maker when required by police may result in a fine up to six hundred rupees.
examples: Printing promotional coupons that closely resemble currency notes.
Distributing imitation bank notes likely to deceive the public.

faqs.question: Can a person whose name appears on such a document be presumed responsible?
faqs.answer: Yes, unless the contrary is proved, it may be presumed that the person caused the document to be made.

title: Effacing Writing from Substance Bearing Government Stamp or Removing Used Stamp
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently removes writing from a stamped document or removes a used Government revenue stamp for reuse on another document commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prevents the fraudulent reuse of Government revenue stamps by removing them from documents or erasing the documents on which they were used.
punishment: Imprisonment up to three years, or fine, or both.
examples: Removing a used revenue stamp from one agreement to use on another.
Erasing a document while retaining the attached revenue stamp for reuse.

faqs.question: Why is reuse of revenue stamps prohibited?
faqs.answer: Because it causes loss of Government revenue and facilitates fraud.

title: Using Government Stamp Known to Have Been Before Used
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever fraudulently uses a Government revenue stamp knowing it has already been used commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes the reuse of previously used Government revenue stamps.
punishment: Imprisonment up to two years, or fine, or both.
examples: Using a previously cancelled revenue stamp on a fresh legal document.
Submitting a document with a reused Government stamp.

faqs.question: Must the person know the stamp was previously used?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of prior use is an essential element of the offence.

title: Erasure of Mark Denoting That Stamp Has Been Used
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever erases or removes marks indicating a Government revenue stamp has been used, or knowingly possesses, sells, or disposes of such stamps, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits tampering with cancellation marks on Government stamps to make them appear unused.
punishment: Imprisonment up to three years, or fine, or both.
examples: Removing cancellation marks from a revenue stamp for reuse.
Selling revenue stamps known to have already been used.

faqs.question: Is merely possessing such altered stamps an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the person knowingly possesses stamps from which usage marks have been erased or removed.

title: Prohibition of Fictitious Stamps
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever makes, sells, uses, possesses, or deals in fictitious postage stamps, or possesses materials for making them without lawful excuse, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits the manufacture, possession, use, or sale of fake postage stamps and related equipment.
punishment: Fine up to two hundred rupees. Fictitious stamps and related equipment may be seized and forfeited.
examples: Printing fake postage stamps resembling official postal stamps.
Possessing plates used to manufacture fictitious stamps without lawful authority.

faqs.question: What is a fictitious stamp?
faqs.answer: A stamp falsely purporting to be issued by Government for postage purposes, including imitations and facsimiles.

title: Person Employed in Mint Causing Coin to Be of Different Weight or Composition
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, being employed in a lawfully established mint, intentionally causes coins issued from that mint to differ in weight or composition from that fixed by law commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes mint employees who deliberately alter the legal standards of coins produced at a mint.
punishment: Imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
examples: Reducing the precious metal content of coins being minted.
Producing coins with a weight lower than the legally prescribed standard.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to mint employees?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence specifically applies to persons employed in a lawfully established mint.

title: Unlawfully Taking Coining Instrument from Mint
chapter: Chapter X - Of Offences Relating to Coin, Currency-Notes, Bank-Notes, and Government Stamps
officialText: Whoever, without lawful authority, removes any coining tool or instrument from a lawfully established mint commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits unauthorized removal of coin-making equipment from a mint.
punishment: Imprisonment up to seven years and fine.
examples: Removing a coin die from a Government mint without permission.
Taking mint equipment for unauthorized use.

faqs.question: Why are mint instruments specially protected?
faqs.answer: Because they can be used for unauthorized coin production and counterfeiting.

title: Unlawful Assembly
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: An assembly of five or more persons is an unlawful assembly if its common object is to overawe Government, resist law, commit offences, obtain property by criminal force, or compel persons by criminal force to do or omit acts against their legal rights.
simpleExplanation: This section defines unlawful assembly and prescribes punishments for joining, remaining in, supporting, organizing, hiring persons for, or participating in such assemblies.
punishment: Varies by conduct: imprisonment up to six months, two years, or fine, or both, depending on the nature of participation and whether deadly weapons are involved.
examples: A group gathering to forcibly seize another person's property.
Remaining in an unlawful assembly after being ordered by authorities to disperse.
Hiring people to participate in a violent mob.

faqs.question: How many people are required for an unlawful assembly?
faqs.answer: Five or more persons.

faqs.question: Can a lawful assembly become unlawful later?
faqs.answer: Yes. An assembly that was lawful when formed may subsequently become unlawful if its common object changes.

title: Every Member of Unlawful Assembly Guilty of Offence Committed in Prosecution of Common Object
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: If an offence is committed by any member of an unlawful assembly in prosecution of the common object of that assembly, or an offence that members knew was likely to be committed, every member of the assembly is guilty of that offence.
simpleExplanation: All members of an unlawful assembly can be held responsible for offences committed by any member if those offences were connected to the group's common objective or were reasonably foreseeable.
punishment: Punishment is the same as that prescribed for the offence committed.
examples: A member of an unlawful mob damages property while pursuing the group's objective; all members may be liable.
An unlawful assembly gathers to forcibly seize land and one member assaults a person during the process.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished even if they did not personally commit the offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Membership in the unlawful assembly is sufficient if the offence was committed in furtherance of the common object.

title: Rioting
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whenever force or violence is used by an unlawful assembly, or by any member thereof, in prosecution of the common object of the assembly, every member of such assembly is guilty of rioting.
simpleExplanation: When an unlawful assembly uses force or violence to achieve its objective, all members become guilty of rioting.
punishment: Rioting: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both. Rioting while armed with a deadly weapon: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A violent mob attacks a building during a protest.
Members of an unlawful assembly carry deadly weapons and engage in violence.

faqs.question: Does every member become liable for rioting?
faqs.answer: Yes. If force or violence is used in furtherance of the common object, every member of the unlawful assembly is guilty of rioting.

title: Wantonly Giving Provocation with Intent to Cause Riot
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever illegally and maliciously gives provocation intending or knowing it is likely to cause rioting commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes persons who intentionally provoke others into committing a riot.
punishment: If rioting occurs: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both. If rioting does not occur: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: A person deliberately incites a crowd into violent action.
Spreading inflammatory messages intended to trigger mob violence.

faqs.question: Is punishment applicable even if no riot takes place?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law punishes the act of intentional provocation even when rioting does not occur.

title: Liability of Owner, Occupier, etc., of Land on Which an Unlawful Assembly or Riot Takes Place
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Owners, occupiers, agents, managers, or beneficiaries of land where an unlawful assembly or riot occurs may be punished if they fail to report, prevent, suppress, or disperse the unlawful activity despite having knowledge or reasonable grounds to believe it would occur.
simpleExplanation: Landowners and persons in control of property may be held responsible if they knowingly allow riots or unlawful assemblies to occur and fail to take lawful steps to stop them.
punishment: Fine up to one thousand rupees.
examples: A landowner allows an unlawful gathering to continue despite knowing it may turn violent.
A manager fails to inform authorities about an impending riot on the property.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished if the riot benefited them?
faqs.answer: Yes. Beneficiaries who fail to prevent or suppress the riot may be liable.

title: Affray
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: When two or more persons fight in a public place and disturb public peace, they commit an affray.
simpleExplanation: A public fight between two or more persons that disturbs public order is an offence called affray.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to one thousand rupees, or both.
examples: Two groups engage in a street fight causing panic among bystanders.
Individuals physically fight in a public marketplace.

faqs.question: Does the fight have to occur in public?
faqs.answer: Yes. An affray requires fighting in a public place and disturbance of public peace.

title: Assaulting or Obstructing Public Servant When Suppressing Riot, etc.
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever assaults, obstructs, threatens, or uses criminal force against a public servant engaged in dispersing an unlawful assembly or suppressing a riot or affray commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section protects public servants who are performing duties related to maintaining public order.
punishment: Assault or criminal force: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine not less than ₹25,000, or both. Threat or attempt: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Attacking a police officer attempting to disperse a violent crowd.
Threatening an officer during a riot-control operation.

faqs.question: Does merely threatening a public servant attract punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Threatening or attempting to obstruct a public servant is punishable.

title: Promoting Enmity Between Different Groups and Doing Acts Prejudicial to Maintenance of Harmony
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever promotes disharmony, enmity, hatred, or ill-will between groups on grounds such as religion, race, language, caste, community, residence, or place of birth, or engages in activities prejudicial to public harmony, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits speech, acts, or organized activities that create hatred, fear, hostility, or communal tension between different groups.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both. If committed in a place of worship or religious assembly: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Publishing content intended to create hatred between religious communities.
Organizing violent training activities targeting a particular community.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to electronic communications?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to spoken, written, visual, electronic, and other forms of communication.

title: Imputations and Assertions Prejudicial to National Integration
chapter: Chapter XI - Of Offences Against the Public Tranquillity
officialText: Whoever makes or publishes imputations, assertions, appeals, or false information that promote disharmony, deny constitutional rights to groups, question their allegiance, or jeopardize the sovereignty, unity, integrity, or security of India commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes statements and publications that undermine national integration, encourage hostility among groups, or spread false information threatening India's unity and security.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both. If committed in a place of worship or religious assembly: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.
examples: Publishing false claims that a community cannot be loyal to India.
Spreading misleading information that threatens national unity.

faqs.question: Can false information affecting India's sovereignty be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Publishing false or misleading information that jeopardizes India's sovereignty, unity, integrity, or security is punishable.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Law with Intent to Cause Injury to Any Person
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys any direction of law governing the manner in which he is required to act, intending or knowing that such disobedience is likely to cause injury to any person, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who deliberately violates legal duties and causes harm or potential harm to a person can be punished under this section.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: An officer deliberately ignores a court order causing loss to a person.
A public servant intentionally violates legal procedures to harm someone.

faqs.question: Is mere negligence sufficient?
faqs.answer: No. The public servant must knowingly disobey the law with intent or knowledge that injury is likely.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Direction Under Law in Investigation
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: A public servant who knowingly violates legal directions relating to investigations, improperly requires attendance, conducts investigations contrary to law, or fails to record information relating to specified cognizable offences commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes public servants who deliberately violate legal safeguards during investigations or fail to register information relating to specified serious offences.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment not less than 6 months and up to 2 years, and fine.
examples: A police officer refuses to record information regarding a specified cognizable offence.
An investigating officer knowingly violates legal procedures to the prejudice of a person.

faqs.question: What is the minimum punishment under this section?
faqs.answer: Rigorous imprisonment of six months.

title: Hospital Authorities Contravening Provisions of Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being in charge of a public or private hospital, contravenes the provisions of section 397 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Hospital authorities are required to comply with legal obligations under the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, and failure to do so attracts punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: A hospital refuses to comply with statutory obligations imposed under Section 397 of BNSS.
A hospital administrator deliberately ignores mandatory legal requirements.

faqs.question: Does this apply to private hospitals?
faqs.answer: Yes. The provision applies to both public and private hospitals.

title: Public Servant Framing an Incorrect Document with Intent to Cause Injury
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant charged with preparing or translating any document or electronic record, knowingly prepares, frames, or translates it incorrectly intending or knowing that such act is likely to cause injury to any person, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant cannot deliberately create, alter, or translate official documents incorrectly in a way that harms another person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A revenue officer intentionally records incorrect land ownership details.
A government translator deliberately mistranslates an official document to harm a citizen.

faqs.question: Is an honest mistake punishable under this section?
faqs.answer: No. The public servant must knowingly prepare or translate the document incorrectly with intent or knowledge that injury may result.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Engaging in Trade
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant and legally prohibited from engaging in trade, engages in trade commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Certain public servants are barred by law from conducting business or trade activities. Violation of that restriction is punishable.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both, or community service.
examples: A government officer secretly operating a private business despite legal restrictions.
A public servant actively participating in commercial trading prohibited by service rules.

faqs.question: Does this apply to all public servants?
faqs.answer: Only to those who are legally prohibited from engaging in trade.

title: Public Servant Unlawfully Buying or Bidding for Property
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally prohibited from purchasing or bidding for certain property, purchases or bids for such property directly or indirectly commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Public servants cannot acquire certain properties when prohibited by law, either in their own name or through another person.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both. The property purchased is liable to confiscation.
examples: A public auction officer secretly purchasing property being auctioned by his department.
A public servant bidding through a relative for property he is prohibited from acquiring.

faqs.question: Can the property be confiscated?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any property purchased in violation of this section may be confiscated.

title: Personating a Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever falsely pretends to hold a public office or impersonates a public servant and performs or attempts to perform acts under the colour of that office commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes people who falsely claim to be government officials and act as if they possess official authority.
punishment: Imprisonment not less than 6 months and up to 3 years, and fine.
examples: Pretending to be a police officer and conducting fake inspections.
Claiming to be a government official to obtain benefits from citizens.

faqs.question: Is merely pretending to be a public servant sufficient?
faqs.answer: The person must also do or attempt to do an act under the assumed authority of that office.

title: Wearing Garb or Carrying Token Used by Public Servant with Fraudulent Intent
chapter: Chapter XII - Of Offences By or Relating to Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, not belonging to a class of public servants, wears their uniform or carries tokens resembling those used by them with intent to deceive others into believing he belongs to that class commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits unauthorized use of official uniforms, badges, insignia, or symbols to create a false impression of official authority.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Wearing a police uniform to gain access to restricted areas.
Using a fake government identification badge to mislead the public.

faqs.question: Is wearing a public servant's uniform always an offence?
faqs.answer: No. The offence requires an intention to deceive or knowledge that others are likely to believe the person is a public servant.

title: Absconding to Avoid Service of Summons or Other Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever absconds to avoid service of a summons, notice, or order issued by a competent public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot deliberately hide or evade authorities to avoid receiving official notices, summonses, or court-related orders.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both. If related to court attendance or production of documents: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: A person goes into hiding to avoid receiving a court summons.
An accused avoids service of a notice requiring production of documents.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to court summons?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to summonses, notices, and orders issued by competent public servants.

title: Preventing Service of Summons or Other Proceeding, or Preventing Publication Thereof
