chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever intentionally prevents service, affixing, delivery, or publication of a summons, notice, order, or proclamation issued by a competent public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes interference with the lawful delivery or publication of official legal communications.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both. If related to court attendance or production of documents: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: Removing a lawfully affixed court notice from a building.
Preventing a process server from delivering a summons.

faqs.question: Can preventing publication of a proclamation be punished?
faqs.answer: Yes. Intentionally preventing lawful publication of a proclamation is an offence.

title: Non-Attendance in Obedience to an Order from Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to attend before a public servant or court pursuant to a summons, notice, order, or proclamation, intentionally fails to attend or leaves before being lawfully permitted to do so commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to appear before an authority or court must attend as directed.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both. For court attendance: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: A witness intentionally fails to appear before a court despite receiving a valid summons.
A person leaves a proceeding before being permitted to do so.

faqs.question: Does this apply to witnesses?
faqs.answer: Yes. Witnesses summoned by a court are legally bound to attend.

title: Non-Appearance in Response to a Proclamation Under Section 84 of Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever fails to appear at the specified place and time as required by a proclamation issued under Section 84 of the Bharatiya Nagarik Suraksha Sanhita, 2023 commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person declared under a legal proclamation must appear as directed. Failure to do so attracts punishment, with harsher penalties for proclaimed offenders.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both, or community service. If declared a proclaimed offender: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: A proclaimed person fails to appear before the court despite public notification.
An accused ignores a proclamation requiring appearance before authorities.

faqs.question: What happens if a person is declared a proclaimed offender?
faqs.answer: The maximum punishment increases to imprisonment up to seven years along with fine.

title: Omission to Produce Document or Electronic Record to Public Servant by Person Legally Bound to Produce It
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to produce or deliver any document or electronic record to a public servant, intentionally omits to do so commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to provide a document or electronic record to a public servant or court cannot intentionally refuse or fail to provide it.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both. If the document is required by a Court: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: Failing to produce financial records demanded by a government authority.
Ignoring a court order to submit a required document.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to both physical documents and electronic records.

title: Omission to Give Notice or Information to Public Servant by Person Legally Bound to Give It
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to give notice or furnish information to a public servant, intentionally omits to do so in the manner and time required by law commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to inform authorities about certain matters must provide the information as required by law.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both. If related to an offence or apprehension of an offender: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both. For violations relating to orders under Section 394 BNSS: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Failing to report a crime when legally required to do so.
Not providing mandatory information sought by lawful authority.

faqs.question: Does failure to report information about a crime attract greater punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Higher punishment applies when the information relates to an offence or apprehension of an offender.

title: Furnishing False Information
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to furnish information to a public servant, knowingly provides information that is false commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to provide information to authorities cannot knowingly give false information.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both. If the information relates to an offence, prevention of an offence, or apprehension of an offender: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Knowingly reporting a murder as an accidental death.
Giving false information to police regarding the location of criminals.

faqs.question: Does this section cover false information relating to serious offences committed outside India?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Explanation extends the meaning of 'offence' to specified serious acts committed outside India that would be punishable if committed in India.

title: Refusing Oath or Affirmation When Duly Required by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever refuses to bind himself by an oath or affirmation to state the truth when lawfully required by a competent public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot refuse to take an oath or affirmation when legally required to do so by an authorized public servant.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Refusing to take an oath before giving evidence to an authorized officer.
Declining to affirm the truth of statements during an official proceeding.

faqs.question: Must the public servant be legally authorized to administer the oath?
faqs.answer: Yes. The requirement must come from a public servant legally competent to demand it.

title: Refusing to Answer Public Servant Authorised to Question
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to state the truth to a public servant, refuses to answer a question lawfully asked by such public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who is legally required to answer questions from an authorized public servant cannot refuse to answer them.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Refusing to answer lawful questions during an official inquiry.
Declining to provide information required during an authorized investigation.

faqs.question: Can any public servant ask questions under this section?
faqs.answer: No. The public servant must be legally authorized to ask the questions.

title: Refusing to Sign Statement
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever refuses to sign a statement made by him when lawfully required to do so by a competent public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot refuse to sign a statement they have made when legally required by an authorized public servant.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹3,000, or both.
examples: Refusing to sign a statement recorded during an official proceeding.
Declining to authenticate one's own declaration before an authorized officer.

faqs.question: Does this section apply only to statements made by the person himself?
faqs.answer: Yes. The statement must be one that was made by the person refusing to sign it.

title: False Statement on Oath or Affirmation to Public Servant or Authorised Person
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound by oath or affirmation to state the truth, makes a statement which is false and which he knows, believes, or does not believe to be true commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Giving false information under oath or affirmation to a public servant or other authorized person is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Giving knowingly false testimony under oath.
Making a false sworn declaration before an authorized officer.

faqs.question: Is an incorrect statement punishable if made honestly?
faqs.answer: No. The person must know, believe, or have reason to believe that the statement is false.

title: False Information with Intent to Cause Public Servant to Use Lawful Power to Injury of Another Person
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever gives false information to a public servant intending or knowing that it is likely to cause the public servant to misuse lawful powers to the injury or annoyance of another person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes people who knowingly provide false information to authorities in order to cause harm, inconvenience, or legal action against another person.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: Making a false complaint to get someone dismissed from service.
Providing fake information to police to trigger an unnecessary search of another person's premises.

faqs.question: Must the false information actually cause harm?
faqs.answer: No. It is sufficient that the person intended or knew it was likely to cause misuse of official power.

title: Resistance to Taking of Property by Lawful Authority of Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever resists the lawful taking of property by a public servant, knowing or having reason to believe that the person is acting in an official capacity, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot obstruct a public servant who is lawfully seizing or taking possession of property under legal authority.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: Preventing an authorized officer from seizing property under a lawful order.
Using force to stop a legally authorized confiscation.

faqs.question: Does the offender need to know that the person is a public servant?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offender must know or have reason to believe that the person is acting as a public servant.

title: Obstructing Sale of Property Offered for Sale by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever intentionally obstructs the sale of property being sold under the lawful authority of a public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot interfere with or prevent an official auction or sale conducted by lawful authority.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Disrupting a government auction to prevent bids.
Preventing prospective buyers from participating in a lawful sale.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to court-ordered auctions?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to any sale conducted under lawful authority of a public servant.

title: Illegal Purchase or Bidding at Sale by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever purchases or bids for property at an official sale on behalf of a person legally disqualified from purchasing it, or bids without intending to fulfill the obligations of the bid, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prohibits fraudulent bidding and bidding on behalf of persons who are legally barred from participating in a sale.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹200, or both.
examples: Bidding at an auction on behalf of a person prohibited from purchasing the property.
Making bids without any intention of paying the bid amount.

faqs.question: Can a person bid for someone legally barred from purchasing the property?
faqs.answer: No. Doing so constitutes an offence under this section.

title: Obstructing Public Servant in Discharge of Public Functions
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever voluntarily obstructs any public servant in the discharge of his public functions commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot intentionally interfere with a public servant while the public servant is performing official duties.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹2,500, or both.
examples: Preventing a government inspector from carrying out an inspection.
Blocking a public servant from executing official duties.

faqs.question: Is physical force necessary to constitute obstruction?
faqs.answer: No. Any voluntary act that obstructs a public servant's lawful functions may constitute the offence.

title: Omission to Assist Public Servant When Bound by Law to Give Assistance
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound to assist a public servant in the execution of public duty, intentionally fails to provide such assistance commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: People who are legally required to help public servants in certain situations cannot refuse or neglect that duty.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 1 month, or fine up to ₹2,500, or both. Where assistance is required for court process, preventing offences, suppressing riots or apprehending offenders: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Refusing to assist police in apprehending an offender when legally required.
Ignoring a lawful request to help suppress a riot.

faqs.question: Does every citizen have a duty to assist public servants?
faqs.answer: Only where the law specifically imposes such a duty.

title: Disobedience to Order Duly Promulgated by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever knowingly disobeys an order lawfully promulgated by a public servant directing abstention from an act or regulating property under his possession or management commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person must obey lawful orders issued by authorized public servants. Violations that create risks to public safety or order are punishable.
punishment: If disobedience causes obstruction, annoyance, injury, or risk thereof: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹2,500, or both. If it causes danger to life, health, safety, or tends to cause a riot or affray: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Ignoring a prohibitory order restricting entry into a dangerous area.
Disobeying an order regulating a public procession and causing a risk of riot.

faqs.question: Must the offender intend to cause harm?
faqs.answer: No. It is sufficient that the person knew of the order and the disobedience caused or was likely to cause harm.

title: Threat of Injury to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever threatens injury to a public servant or a person in whom the public servant is interested in order to influence the exercise of the public servant's official functions commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Threatening a public servant or someone connected to them to influence official decisions is prohibited.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening a tax officer to stop an investigation.
Threatening harm to a public servant's family member to influence an official decision.

faqs.question: Must the threat be carried out to constitute the offence?
faqs.answer: No. Making the threat for the prohibited purpose is sufficient.

title: Threat of Injury to Induce Person to Refrain from Applying for Protection to Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever threatens injury to any person in order to prevent that person from seeking lawful protection from a public servant commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: No one may threaten another person to stop them from seeking legal protection or assistance from authorities.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening a victim to prevent them from filing a complaint with the police.
Intimidating a witness to stop them from seeking protection from authorities.

faqs.question: Does the person actually need to refrain from seeking protection?
faqs.answer: No. The offence is complete when the threat is made for that purpose.

title: Attempt to Commit Suicide to Compel or Restrain Exercise of Lawful Power
chapter: Chapter XIII - Of Contempts of the Lawful Authority of Public Servants
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit suicide with the intent to compel or restrain a public servant from performing official duties commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Attempting suicide as a means of coercing a public servant into acting or refraining from acting is punishable.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both, or community service.
examples: Threatening self-harm to stop a lawful eviction by public authorities.
Attempting suicide to pressure a public servant into withdrawing official action.

faqs.question: Is every suicide attempt covered by this section?
faqs.answer: No. The attempt must be intended to compel or restrain a public servant in the exercise of official powers.

title: Giving False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being legally bound by oath, law, or declaration to state the truth, makes a statement that is false and which he knows, believes, or does not believe to be true, is said to give false evidence.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when knowingly making a false statement while under a legal obligation to tell the truth.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under subsequent provisions relating to false evidence.
examples: Knowingly giving false testimony in court.
Making a false declaration under oath.

faqs.question: Can a person commit this offence by falsely stating a belief?
faqs.answer: Yes. Falsely claiming to believe something that one does not actually believe amounts to false evidence.

title: Fabricating False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever creates circumstances, false entries, records, documents, or electronic records intending them to be used as evidence and to mislead a judicial authority or public servant commits the offence of fabricating false evidence.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes the creation of fake evidence intended to influence legal or official proceedings.
punishment: Punishment is prescribed under Section 229.
examples: Planting stolen property to falsely implicate someone.
Creating fake accounting entries for use in court proceedings.

faqs.question: Does creating a false document for court use amount to fabrication of evidence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any false document intended to mislead a judicial or official proceeding may constitute fabricated evidence.

title: Punishment for False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally gives or fabricates false evidence in a judicial proceeding or for use in such proceeding commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section prescribes punishment for perjury and fabrication of evidence, with higher penalties when connected to judicial proceedings.
punishment: Judicial proceedings: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine up to ₹10,000. Other cases: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹5,000.
examples: Giving false testimony during a court trial.
Creating forged documents for submission in a judicial proceeding.

faqs.question: Does an investigation ordered by a court count as a judicial proceeding?
faqs.answer: Yes. Investigations directed by law or by a court are treated as stages of a judicial proceeding.

title: False Evidence Intended to Procure Conviction of Capital Offence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending to cause, or knowing it is likely to cause, the conviction of a person for an offence punishable with death commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Providing false evidence to secure the conviction of someone for a capital offence is treated as an extremely serious crime.
punishment: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine up to ₹50,000. If an innocent person is convicted and executed as a result, the offender may be punished with death or with the punishment specified above.
examples: Fabricating evidence to falsely implicate someone in a murder case.
Giving knowingly false testimony that could result in a death sentence.

faqs.question: What if the innocent person is executed because of the false evidence?
faqs.answer: The person who gave or fabricated the false evidence may be punished with death or life imprisonment.

title: Giving or Fabricating False Evidence with Intent to Procure Conviction of Offence Punishable with Life Imprisonment or Long-Term Imprisonment
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever gives or fabricates false evidence intending or knowing it is likely to cause the conviction of a person for an offence punishable with imprisonment for life or imprisonment for seven years or more shall be punished as if convicted of that offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who falsely implicates another in a serious crime through false evidence can receive the same punishment that the innocent person would have faced.
punishment: Same punishment as prescribed for the offence for which the false conviction was intended.
examples: Fabricating evidence to falsely implicate someone in dacoity.
Giving false testimony to secure a conviction for a serious offence carrying life imprisonment.

faqs.question: Can the offender receive the same punishment as the falsely accused person?
faqs.answer: Yes. The law treats the offender as if convicted of the offence for which the false conviction was sought.

title: Threatening Any Person to Give False Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever threatens another person with injury to person, reputation, or property in order to induce that person to give false evidence commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Threatening someone to force them to lie in legal proceedings is a serious criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both. If an innocent person is convicted and sentenced to death or imprisonment exceeding seven years because of such false evidence, the offender receives the same punishment as the innocent person.
examples: Threatening a witness to provide false testimony in court.
Intimidating a person into signing a false statement.

faqs.question: What if the threat results in an innocent person being convicted?
faqs.answer: The offender may receive the same punishment imposed on the innocent person.

title: Using Evidence Known to be False
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as genuine any evidence which he knows to be false or fabricated commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly presenting fake evidence as genuine is punished in the same way as creating or giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as for giving or fabricating false evidence.
examples: Submitting forged documents in court while knowing they are fake.
Presenting fabricated records during a legal proceeding.

faqs.question: Must the person know the evidence is false?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge that the evidence is false or fabricated is essential.

title: Issuing or Signing False Certificate
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever issues or signs a certificate required by law, knowing or believing it to be false in any material particular, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Issuing an official certificate containing knowingly false information is treated like giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as for giving false evidence.
examples: Issuing a false medical certificate.
Signing a certificate containing knowingly incorrect information.

faqs.question: Does the falsehood need to be material?
faqs.answer: Yes. The false statement must relate to a material point in the certificate.

title: Using as True a Certificate Known to be False
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use a certificate as genuine, knowing it to be false in any material point, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Using a false certificate as though it were genuine is punishable in the same manner as giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as for giving false evidence.
examples: Submitting a forged educational certificate for employment.
Using a false medical certificate in a legal proceeding.

faqs.question: Is mere possession of a false certificate enough?
faqs.answer: No. The person must use or attempt to use it as genuine.

title: False Statement Made in Declaration Receivable as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever makes a false statement in a declaration that is receivable as evidence by a court, public servant, or other authorized person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Making false statements in declarations that can be used as evidence is treated the same as giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as for giving false evidence.
examples: Making a false declaration in a statutory affidavit.
Providing incorrect information in a legally admissible declaration.

faqs.question: Must the false statement relate to an important fact?
faqs.answer: Yes. It must concern a material point relevant to the purpose of the declaration.

title: Using as True a Declaration Known to be False
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever corruptly uses or attempts to use as true any declaration knowing it to be false in a material point commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person who knowingly relies on a false declaration as genuine is punishable in the same way as someone giving false evidence.
punishment: Same punishment as for giving false evidence.
examples: Submitting a false declaration to a government authority.
Using a false affidavit in support of a legal claim.

faqs.question: Does an informal declaration fall within this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even a declaration with procedural defects may be covered if it is otherwise receivable as evidence.

title: Causing Disappearance of Evidence of Offence or Giving False Information to Screen Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever causes evidence of an offence to disappear or gives false information about an offence with the intention of shielding the offender from punishment commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Destroying evidence or misleading authorities to help an offender escape punishment is a serious crime.
punishment: If the offence is punishable with death: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine. If punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine. For other offences: imprisonment up to one-fourth of the maximum term prescribed for that offence, or fine, or both.
examples: Helping a murderer hide a victim's body.
Destroying documents to prevent prosecution of an offender.

faqs.question: Is giving false information to protect an offender covered?
faqs.answer: Yes. Both destruction of evidence and false information intended to shield an offender are punishable.

title: Intentional Omission to Give Information of Offence by Person Bound to Inform
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever knowingly omits to provide information regarding an offence when legally required to do so commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: People who are legally required to report offences must do so; deliberately withholding such information is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: A person legally obligated to report a serious crime deliberately remains silent.
Failing to inform authorities about an offence despite a legal duty to report.

faqs.question: Is mere knowledge of an offence sufficient for liability?
faqs.answer: The person must also be under a legal obligation to provide the information.

title: Giving False Information Respecting an Offence Committed
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, knowing or having reason to believe that an offence has been committed, gives information regarding that offence which he knows or believes to be false commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Providing false information about a crime to authorities is punishable under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Knowingly providing a false alibi for an offender.
Misleading police about the circumstances of a crime.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to offences committed in India?
faqs.answer: No. The section also covers certain serious acts committed outside India that would be punishable if committed in India.

title: Destruction of Document or Electronic Record to Prevent Its Production as Evidence
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever secretes, destroys, obliterates, or renders illegible any document or electronic record that may lawfully be required as evidence before a Court or public servant, with intent to prevent its production or use as evidence, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Destroying, hiding, altering, or making evidence unreadable to prevent its use in legal proceedings is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Deleting electronic records after receiving a court notice.
Destroying a contract to prevent it from being produced in court.

faqs.question: Does this section apply to electronic records?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies equally to documents and electronic records.

title: False Personation for Purpose of Act or Proceeding in Suit or Prosecution
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever falsely personates another person and, in that assumed character, makes admissions, statements, confesses judgment, becomes bail or security, or performs any act in a suit or criminal prosecution, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be another person during legal proceedings is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Appearing in court pretending to be another litigant.
Falsely acting as another person's surety in a criminal case.

faqs.question: Must the impersonation occur in connection with legal proceedings?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence relates to acts performed in suits or criminal prosecutions.

title: Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property to Prevent Its Seizure as Forfeited or in Execution
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently removes, conceals, transfers, or delivers property to prevent its forfeiture, seizure, or execution under a court decree, order, or sentence commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot hide or transfer property to avoid court-ordered forfeiture, fines, or execution of decrees.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Transferring property to relatives to avoid court seizure.
Concealing assets before execution of a court decree.

faqs.question: Does this apply before a decree is actually passed?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies if the person knows such a decree or sentence is likely to be passed.

title: Fraudulent Claim to Property to Prevent Its Seizure as Forfeited or in Execution
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently accepts, receives, claims, or deceives regarding property to prevent its forfeiture or seizure under legal process commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Making false claims over property to stop lawful seizure or forfeiture is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Claiming ownership of property to shield it from attachment.
Receiving property fraudulently to prevent its seizure.

faqs.question: Must the claimant know they have no lawful right?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge of the lack of a rightful claim is essential.

title: Fraudulently Suffering Decree for Sum Not Due
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently causes or permits a decree or order to be passed for a sum not due, a larger sum than due, or property not legally claimable, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Allowing or arranging false court decrees to defeat legitimate claims or mislead the court is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Allowing a false decree to be passed to shield assets from creditors.
Permitting execution of a decree that has already been satisfied.

faqs.question: Can a person be liable for allowing a false decree against themselves?
faqs.answer: Yes. Fraudulently suffering such a decree is specifically covered.

title: Dishonestly Making False Claim in Court
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently, dishonestly, or with intent to injure or annoy any person, makes in a Court a claim which he knows to be false, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Knowingly filing a false claim in court is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
examples: Filing a suit claiming ownership of property one knows belongs to another.
Making a false monetary claim against a person in court.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of falsity required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The claimant must know the claim is false.

title: Fraudulently Obtaining Decree for Sum Not Due
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever fraudulently obtains a decree or order for a sum not due, a larger sum than due, or property to which he is not entitled, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Obtaining court orders or decrees through fraud for money or property not lawfully owed is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Obtaining a decree for money never owed.
Executing a decree after the debt has already been satisfied.

faqs.question: Can liability arise from allowing someone else to act fraudulently in one's name?
faqs.answer: Yes. Permitting such acts in one's name is also covered.

title: False Charge of Offence Made with Intent to Injure
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever institutes criminal proceedings or falsely charges another person with an offence, knowing there is no lawful basis for such charge, with intent to cause injury, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Falsely accusing someone of a crime to harm them is punishable.
punishment: General case: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine up to ₹2 lakh, or both. If the false charge concerns an offence punishable with death, life imprisonment, or imprisonment of 10 years or more: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Filing a fabricated criminal complaint against a rival.
Knowingly accusing an innocent person of murder.

faqs.question: What if the false accusation concerns a very serious offence?
faqs.answer: The punishment increases to imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

title: Harbouring Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever harbours or conceals a person known or believed to be an offender with the intention of shielding that person from legal punishment commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Helping offenders hide from law enforcement is punishable.
punishment: If the offence is punishable with death: imprisonment up to 5 years and fine. If punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine. For lesser offences: imprisonment up to one-fourth of the maximum term prescribed for the offence, or fine, or both.
examples: Hiding a dacoit to prevent arrest.
Providing shelter to a fugitive knowing he committed a serious offence.

faqs.question: Does this section apply when the offender is the spouse of the accused person?
faqs.answer: No. Harbouring or concealing one's spouse is expressly exempted.

title: Taking Gratification to Screen Offender from Punishment
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever accepts, attempts to obtain, or agrees to accept any gratification or restoration of property in consideration of concealing an offence, screening an offender, or not prosecuting an offender commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Accepting money, benefits, or property in exchange for helping an offender avoid punishment is punishable.
punishment: If the offence is punishable with death: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine. If punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine. For lesser offences: imprisonment up to one-fourth of the maximum punishment prescribed for the offence, or fine, or both.
examples: Accepting money to avoid reporting a murder.
Receiving property in exchange for not filing a criminal complaint.

faqs.question: Does merely agreeing to accept gratification constitute the offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Even agreeing to accept such gratification is punishable.

title: Offering Gift or Restoration of Property in Consideration of Screening Offender
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever gives, offers, agrees to give any gratification or restores property in consideration of concealing an offence, screening an offender from punishment, or refraining from prosecution commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Paying or offering benefits to someone in exchange for hiding a crime or protecting an offender from punishment is illegal.
punishment: If the offence is punishable with death: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine. If punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine. For other offences: imprisonment up to one-fourth of the maximum punishment for that offence, or fine, or both.
examples: Offering money to a witness to conceal a murder.
Returning stolen property in exchange for not filing a criminal complaint.

faqs.question: Does this apply to compoundable offences?
faqs.answer: No. This section and Section 250 do not apply where the offence is lawfully compoundable.

title: Taking Gift to Help Recover Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever accepts gratification for helping recover movable property obtained through an offence, without using all means to secure the offender's apprehension and conviction, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot accept payment for helping recover stolen property while allowing the offender to escape justice.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Accepting money to recover stolen jewelry but refusing to identify the thief.
Negotiating return of stolen property while protecting the offender.

faqs.question: Can a reward be accepted if efforts are made to prosecute the offender?
faqs.answer: Yes, provided all possible efforts are used to ensure the offender is apprehended and convicted.

title: Harbouring Offender Who Has Escaped from Custody or Whose Apprehension Has Been Ordered
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever harbours or conceals a person who has escaped lawful custody or whose arrest has been ordered, knowing such facts and intending to prevent apprehension, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Helping an escaped prisoner or wanted person evade arrest is punishable.
punishment: If the offence is punishable with death: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine. If punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years: imprisonment up to 3 years. For lesser offences: imprisonment up to one-fourth of the maximum punishment for the offence, or fine, or both.
examples: Providing shelter to an escaped murder accused.
Hiding a wanted offender to prevent arrest.

faqs.question: Does the spouse of the offender commit an offence under this section?
faqs.answer: No. Harbouring or concealing by the spouse is exempt.

title: Penalty for Harbouring Robbers or Dacoits
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever harbours persons known or believed to be about to commit or to have committed robbery or dacoity, intending to facilitate the offence or shield them from punishment, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Providing shelter or assistance to robbers or dacoits before or after the crime is a serious offence.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Providing accommodation to a gang planning a dacoity.
Hiding robbers after the commission of a robbery.

faqs.question: Does it matter whether the robbery occurred inside or outside India?
faqs.answer: No. The section applies regardless of where the robbery or dacoity occurred or was intended to occur.

title: Public Servant Disobeying Direction of Law with Intent to Save Person from Punishment or Property from Forfeiture
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, knowingly disobeys legal directions intending or knowing that such disobedience may save a person from punishment or property from forfeiture commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant cannot deliberately ignore the law to help someone avoid punishment or protect property from legal consequences.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A police officer intentionally failing to file charges against an accused person.
A revenue officer ignoring forfeiture proceedings to protect someone's property.

faqs.question: Must the public servant act knowingly?
faqs.answer: Yes. The disobedience must be intentional and with knowledge of its likely effect.

title: Public Servant Framing Incorrect Record or Writing with Intent to Save Person from Punishment or Property from Forfeiture
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant responsible for preparing records or writings, knowingly prepares them incorrectly to cause injury, shield a person from punishment, or save property from forfeiture commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Public servants who intentionally create false records to help offenders or protect property are punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Altering investigation records to help an accused person.
Preparing false official documents to prevent property confiscation.

faqs.question: Is actual harm required for liability?
faqs.answer: No. Intention or knowledge that harm or protection may result is sufficient.

title: Public Servant in Judicial Proceeding Corruptly Making Report, Order, Verdict or Decision Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant, corruptly or maliciously makes or pronounces any report, order, verdict, or decision in a judicial proceeding knowing it to be contrary to law commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Judicial or quasi-judicial officers cannot knowingly issue unlawful decisions for corrupt or malicious reasons.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Knowingly issuing an illegal judicial order in exchange for a bribe.
Passing a verdict contrary to law out of personal malice.

faqs.question: Does a mere legal error amount to this offence?
faqs.answer: No. The public servant must knowingly act contrary to law and do so corruptly or maliciously.

title: Commitment for Trial or Confinement by Person Having Authority Who Knows He Is Acting Contrary to Law
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, having legal authority to commit persons for trial or confinement, knowingly and unlawfully commits or keeps a person in confinement commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Officials with custody powers cannot knowingly order or maintain unlawful detention.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Ordering detention without legal authority.
Keeping a person confined despite knowing there is no lawful basis.

faqs.question: Must the official know the confinement is unlawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. Knowledge that the act is contrary to law is essential.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend on Part of Public Servant Bound to Apprehend
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep a person in confinement, intentionally fails to apprehend, allows escape, or aids escape commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant cannot deliberately fail to arrest or unlawfully assist the escape of an accused person.
punishment: If the accused is charged with an offence punishable with death: imprisonment up to 7 years. If punishable with life imprisonment or up to 10 years: imprisonment up to 3 years. For lesser offences: imprisonment up to 2 years.
examples: A police officer deliberately allowing a murder accused to escape.
Helping an arrested person flee custody.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to police officers?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to any public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep a person in confinement.

title: Intentional Omission to Apprehend on Part of Public Servant Bound to Apprehend Person Under Sentence or Lawfully Committed
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep in confinement a person under sentence or lawfully committed to custody, intentionally fails to do so or assists escape commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who knowingly allows a convicted prisoner or detainee to escape commits a serious offence.
punishment: If the prisoner is under sentence of death: imprisonment for life or imprisonment up to 14 years. If under life imprisonment or imprisonment of 10 years or more: imprisonment up to 7 years. In other cases: imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Allowing a death-row convict to escape.
Helping a sentenced prisoner flee lawful custody.

faqs.question: Is the punishment higher for allowing a death-sentenced prisoner to escape?
faqs.answer: Yes. The punishment may extend to life imprisonment or 14 years.

title: Escape from Confinement Negligently Suffered by Public Servant
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to keep any person in confinement or lawful custody, negligently allows such person to escape commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who carelessly allows a prisoner or detainee to escape from lawful custody can be punished.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A jail officer leaves prison gates unsecured, allowing an inmate to escape.
A police officer negligently supervises a detainee who then escapes custody.

faqs.question: Is intentional assistance required for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. Mere negligence resulting in escape is sufficient.

title: Resistance or Obstruction by a Person to His Lawful Apprehension
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever resists or obstructs his lawful apprehension, or escapes or attempts to escape from lawful custody, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person cannot resist arrest, obstruct lawful apprehension, or escape from lawful custody.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both, in addition to punishment for the original offence.
examples: An accused person struggles with police officers to avoid arrest.
A prisoner attempts to escape from lawful custody.

faqs.question: Does this replace punishment for the original offence?
faqs.answer: No. This punishment is additional to punishment for the original offence.

title: Resistance or Obstruction to Lawful Apprehension of Another Person
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever resists or obstructs the lawful apprehension of another person, or rescues or attempts to rescue another person from lawful custody, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Helping another person avoid arrest or escape from custody is punishable, with penalties depending on the seriousness of the underlying offence.
punishment: Up to 2 years generally; up to 3 years for serious offences; up to 7 years for offences punishable with death or life imprisonment; life imprisonment or up to 10 years if rescuing a person under sentence of death.
examples: Helping a murder accused escape from police custody.
Blocking police officers from arresting a wanted offender.

faqs.question: Does punishment depend on the offence committed by the rescued person?
faqs.answer: Yes. Higher punishments apply where the person is accused or convicted of serious offences.

title: Omission to Apprehend or Sufferance of Escape by Public Servant in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, being a public servant legally bound to apprehend or keep a person in confinement, omits to do so or allows escape in cases not covered by other provisions, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A public servant who intentionally or negligently fails to apprehend or detain a person when legally required may be punished.
punishment: Intentional omission: imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both. Negligent omission: simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A public servant intentionally ignores a lawful duty to detain a person.
A negligent officer allows a detainee to leave custody.

faqs.question: Does this apply only when other specific escape provisions do not?
faqs.answer: Yes. It applies to cases not covered by Sections 259, 260, 261, or other laws.

title: Resistance, Obstruction, Escape or Rescue in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever resists lawful apprehension, escapes from lawful custody, or rescues another person from lawful custody in cases not otherwise provided for commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: This section covers general cases of resisting arrest, escaping custody, or helping others escape where no specific provision applies.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
examples: Escaping from lawful custody in a situation not covered by other sections.
Helping another person evade lawful detention.

faqs.question: Why does this section exist when other escape-related sections exist?
faqs.answer: It serves as a general provision for situations not specifically covered elsewhere.

title: Violation of Condition of Remission of Punishment
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever knowingly violates a condition attached to a remission of punishment shall be required to undergo the original punishment remaining unsuffered.
simpleExplanation: If a person receives a conditional reduction in punishment and breaks the condition, the original punishment may be revived.
punishment: The offender must undergo the original sentence or the remaining part of it not already served.
examples: Violating a condition attached to early release from prison.
Failing to comply with terms of conditional remission.

faqs.question: What happens if part of the sentence has already been served?
faqs.answer: The offender serves the remaining portion of the original sentence.

title: Intentional Insult or Interruption to Public Servant Sitting in Judicial Proceeding
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally insults or interrupts a public servant while the public servant is sitting in any stage of a judicial proceeding commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Disrupting or insulting a judge or other public servant during judicial proceedings is punishable.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Creating disturbances in court proceedings.
Intentionally insulting a magistrate during a hearing.

faqs.question: Must the interruption occur during a judicial proceeding?
faqs.answer: Yes. The public servant must be sitting in a judicial proceeding.

title: Personation of Assessor
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever intentionally causes or allows himself to be returned, empanelled, sworn, or serves as an assessor when not legally entitled to do so commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Pretending to be legally qualified to serve as an assessor in judicial proceedings is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Serving as an assessor despite knowing one is legally disqualified.
Impersonating a qualified assessor.

faqs.question: Is knowledge of ineligibility required?
faqs.answer: Yes. The person must know they are not legally entitled to act as an assessor.

title: Failure by Person Released on Bail Bond or Bond to Appear in Court
chapter: Chapter XIV - Of False Evidence and Offences Against Public Justice
officialText: Whoever, having been released on bail bond or bond, fails without sufficient cause to appear in Court according to the terms of the bond commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A person released on bail must appear before the court as required. Failure to do so without valid reason is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Skipping a scheduled court appearance while on bail.
Failing to appear before the court despite being released on bond.

faqs.question: Who must prove the existence of sufficient cause?
faqs.answer: The burden of proving sufficient cause lies on the accused person.

title: Public Nuisance
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: A person commits public nuisance by doing an act or illegal omission that causes common injury, danger, annoyance, obstruction, or inconvenience to the public or to persons exercising public rights.
simpleExplanation: Acts or omissions that harm, inconvenience, or endanger the public generally are considered public nuisance offences.
punishment: Punishment prescribed in subsequent provisions relating to public nuisance.
examples: Blocking a public road and causing inconvenience to the public.
Creating hazardous conditions that endanger people using public places.

faqs.question: Can a nuisance be excused because it provides some benefit?
faqs.answer: No. A common nuisance is not excused merely because it provides convenience or advantage to some people.

faqs.answer: No. Mere negligence resulting in escape is sufficient.

faqs.answer: Yes. Higher punishments apply where the person is accused or convicted of serious offences.

examples: Escaping from lawful custody in a situation not covered by other sections.
Helping another person evade lawful detention.

faqs.answer: It serves as a general provision for situations not specifically covered elsewhere.

Intentionally insulting a magistrate during a hearing.

examples: Skipping a scheduled court appearance while on bail.
Failing to appear before the court despite being released on bond.

faqs.question: Who must prove the existence of sufficient cause?
faqs.answer: The burden of proving sufficient cause lies on the accused person.

title: Public Nuisance
