officialText: A person commits public nuisance by doing an act or illegal omission that causes common injury, danger, annoyance, obstruction, or inconvenience to the public or to persons exercising public rights.
simpleExplanation: Acts or omissions that harm, inconvenience, or endanger the public generally are considered public nuisance offences.
punishment: Punishment prescribed in subsequent provisions relating to public nuisance.
examples: Blocking a public road and causing inconvenience to the public.
Creating hazardous conditions that endanger people using public places.

faqs.question: Can a nuisance be excused because it provides some benefit?
faqs.answer: No. A common nuisance is not excused merely because it provides convenience or advantage to some people.

title: Rash Driving or Riding on a Public Way
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever drives any vehicle or rides on a public way in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life or likely to cause hurt or injury to any other person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Driving or riding carelessly on a public road in a way that could endanger others is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Driving at excessive speed through a crowded market.
Performing dangerous stunts on a public road.

faqs.question: Is an accident necessary for this offence?
faqs.answer: No. Endangering life or creating the likelihood of injury is sufficient.

title: Rash Navigation of Vessel
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever navigates any vessel in a manner so rash or negligent as to endanger human life or likely to cause hurt or injury to another person commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Operating a boat or vessel carelessly and creating danger to others is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹10,000, or both.
examples: Operating a ferry recklessly in crowded waters.
Ignoring navigation rules and causing danger to passengers.

faqs.question: Does actual injury need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. The likelihood of danger or injury is enough.

title: Exhibition of False Light, Mark or Buoy
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever exhibits any false light, mark or buoy intending or knowing it is likely to mislead navigators commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Placing false navigation signals that may mislead ships or boats is a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine of not less than ₹10,000.
examples: Displaying a fake lighthouse signal.
Placing misleading navigation buoys in a waterway.

faqs.question: Why is this offence serious?
faqs.answer: False navigation signals can cause shipwrecks and loss of life.

title: Conveying Person by Water for Hire in Unsafe or Overloaded Vessel
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly or negligently conveys any person for hire in a vessel that is unsafe or overloaded so as to endanger life commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Transporting passengers in an unsafe or overloaded boat is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Operating an overcrowded passenger boat.
Using an unseaworthy vessel to transport passengers.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to paid transportation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically refers to conveyance for hire.

title: Danger or Obstruction in Public Way or Line of Navigation
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever by any act or omission relating to property under his control causes danger, obstruction or injury in a public way or public line of navigation commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Creating hazards or obstructions in roads or navigable routes is punishable.
punishment: Fine up to ₹5,000.
examples: Leaving construction materials on a public road.
Placing obstacles in a navigable waterway.

faqs.question: Must injury actually occur?
faqs.answer: No. Creating danger or obstruction is sufficient.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Poisonous Substance
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever rashly or negligently handles a poisonous substance or fails to take sufficient precautions regarding such substance commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Improper handling or storage of poisonous substances that may endanger life is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Leaving toxic chemicals unsecured in a public place.
Failing to safely store poisonous pesticides.

faqs.question: Is actual injury required?
faqs.answer: No. The likelihood of danger to human life is enough.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Fire or Combustible Matter
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever rashly or negligently handles fire or combustible matter or fails to take sufficient precautions against danger to human life commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Careless handling of fire or flammable materials that creates risk to others is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹2,000, or both.
examples: Leaving an open fire unattended near flammable materials.
Improper storage of combustible substances.

faqs.question: What kind of materials are covered?
faqs.answer: Any fire or combustible matter capable of causing danger to human life.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Explosive Substance
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever rashly or negligently handles explosive substances or fails to take sufficient precautions to prevent danger from them commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Unsafe handling or storage of explosives that may endanger human life is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Improper storage of fireworks or explosives.
Handling explosives without necessary safety measures.

faqs.question: Does this apply even if no explosion occurs?
faqs.answer: Yes. The likelihood of danger is sufficient.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Machinery
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever rashly or negligently uses machinery or fails to take sufficient precautions against danger from machinery commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Careless use or maintenance of machinery that may endanger human life is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Operating dangerous machinery without safety guards.
Failing to secure industrial equipment properly.

faqs.question: Does this apply to workplace machinery?
faqs.answer: Yes. Any machinery under a person's possession or care is covered.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Pulling Down, Repairing or Constructing Buildings
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever, while pulling down, repairing or constructing a building, knowingly or negligently fails to take sufficient measures to guard against danger to human life commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Construction or demolition work must be carried out safely. Failure to take necessary precautions is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Failing to erect safety barriers around a demolition site.
Allowing loose construction materials to endanger pedestrians.

faqs.question: Does actual collapse need to occur?
faqs.answer: No. The failure to take reasonable safety measures itself is sufficient.

title: Negligent Conduct with Respect to Animal
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever knowingly or negligently omits to take sufficient measures with any animal in his possession to guard against probable danger to human life or grievous hurt commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Owners or handlers of animals must take reasonable precautions to prevent their animals from causing serious harm to people.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Allowing a known aggressive dog to roam freely in a public area.
Failing to properly secure a dangerous animal.

faqs.question: Does the animal actually need to injure someone?
faqs.answer: No. Failure to take precautions against probable danger is sufficient.

title: Punishment for Public Nuisance in Cases Not Otherwise Provided For
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever commits a public nuisance in any case not otherwise punishable under this Sanhita shall be punished.
simpleExplanation: General public nuisance offences not specifically covered elsewhere are punishable under this section.
punishment: Fine up to ₹1,000.
examples: Creating a public nuisance not covered by a specific provision.
Causing inconvenience to the public through unlawful acts.

faqs.question: When does this section apply?
faqs.answer: It applies when no other specific provision provides punishment for the nuisance.

title: Continuance of Nuisance After Injunction to Discontinue
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever repeats or continues a public nuisance after being lawfully directed by a public servant to discontinue it commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Ignoring an official order to stop a public nuisance is punishable.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: Continuing illegal loudspeaker use despite a lawful prohibition order.
Persisting with an activity declared a public nuisance by authorities.

faqs.question: Must there be a prior lawful order?
faqs.answer: Yes. The nuisance must continue after a lawful injunction to discontinue it.

title: Sale, Distribution or Circulation of Obscene Books and Objects
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever sells, distributes, publicly exhibits, imports, exports, advertises, produces, possesses for circulation, or otherwise deals in obscene books, writings, drawings, paintings, representations, electronic content or other obscene objects commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: The law prohibits the commercial production, distribution, circulation, and promotion of obscene material.
punishment: First conviction: imprisonment up to 2 years and fine up to ₹5,000. Subsequent conviction: imprisonment up to 5 years and fine up to ₹10,000.
examples: Selling obscene publications for profit.
Distributing obscene digital content to the public.

faqs.question: Are works of art, science, literature or religion exempt?
faqs.answer: Yes, where publication is justified for public good, science, literature, art, learning, or bona fide religious purposes.

title: Sale or Distribution of Obscene Objects to a Child
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever sells, distributes, exhibits, circulates, offers, or attempts to provide obscene objects to a child commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Providing obscene material to children is treated more seriously than general obscenity offences.
punishment: First conviction: imprisonment up to 3 years and fine up to ₹2,000. Subsequent conviction: imprisonment up to 7 years and fine up to ₹5,000.
examples: Selling obscene magazines to a minor.
Distributing obscene digital content to children.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher under this section?
faqs.answer: Because children are given special protection from exposure to obscene material.

title: Obscene Acts and Songs
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever, to the annoyance of others, does any obscene act in a public place or sings, recites, or utters any obscene song, ballad, or words in or near a public place commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Performing obscene acts or using obscene language in public places is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both.
examples: Performing obscene acts in a public park.
Using obscene songs or language in a public place.

faqs.question: Must other people be annoyed by the conduct?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section specifically requires annoyance to others.

title: Keeping Lottery Office
chapter: Chapter XV - Of Offences Affecting the Public Health, Safety, Convenience, Decency and Morals
officialText: Whoever keeps an office or place for drawing any lottery other than a State lottery or one authorised by the State Government commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Operating or promoting unauthorized lotteries is punishable.
punishment: Keeping lottery office: imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both. Publishing lottery proposals: fine up to ₹5,000.
examples: Running an unauthorized lottery scheme.
Publishing advertisements promoting an illegal lottery.

faqs.question: Are State-authorised lotteries covered?
faqs.answer: No. State lotteries and lotteries authorised by the State Government are exempt.

title: Injuring or Defiling Place of Worship with Intent to Insult Religion
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever destroys, damages, or defiles any place of worship or sacred object with intent to insult the religion of any class of persons, or knowing such act is likely to be considered an insult, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Damaging religious places or sacred objects to insult religious beliefs is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Defacing a place of worship to insult a religious community.
Damaging a sacred religious object intentionally.

faqs.question: Is intention important under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be intended to insult religion or be done with knowledge of its likely effect.

title: Deliberate and Malicious Acts Intended to Outrage Religious Feelings
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever, with deliberate and malicious intention of outraging the religious feelings of any class of citizens, insults or attempts to insult their religion or religious beliefs by words, signs, visible representations, electronic means, or otherwise commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Deliberately insulting religious beliefs with malicious intent is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Publishing material deliberately intended to outrage religious feelings.
Using electronic media to maliciously insult religious beliefs.

faqs.question: Is mere criticism of religion covered?
faqs.answer: The section specifically requires deliberate and malicious intent to outrage religious feelings.

title: Disturbing Religious Assembly
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever voluntarily causes disturbance to any assembly lawfully engaged in religious worship or religious ceremonies commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Disrupting a lawful religious gathering or ceremony is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Interrupting a religious ceremony intentionally.
Creating disturbances during a lawful worship gathering.

faqs.question: Must the assembly be lawful?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies to assemblies lawfully engaged in worship or ceremonies.

title: Trespassing on Burial Places or Offering Indignity to Human Corpse
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever, with intent to wound feelings, insult religion, or with knowledge that such feelings are likely to be wounded, commits trespass in a place of worship, burial place, place of funeral rites, offers indignity to a human corpse, or disturbs funeral ceremonies commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Disrespecting burial places, funeral rites, places of worship, or human remains in a manner that insults religious or personal sentiments is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Disturbing a funeral ceremony to insult mourners.
Defiling a burial ground or disrespecting a human corpse.

faqs.question: Does the offence require intent to insult or wound feelings?
faqs.answer: Yes, or knowledge that such feelings are likely to be hurt.

title: Uttering Words, Gestures or Objects with Deliberate Intent to Wound Religious Feelings
chapter: Chapter XVI - Of Offences Relating to Religion
officialText: Whoever, with the deliberate intention of wounding the religious feelings of any person, utters any word, makes any sound, gesture, or places any object within the sight or hearing of that person, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally using words, sounds, gestures, or objects to hurt another person's religious feelings is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Making insulting gestures aimed at a person's religion.
Displaying an object specifically intended to offend someone's religious beliefs.

faqs.question: Is intention important under this section?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be done with a deliberate intention to wound religious feelings.

title: Theft
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, intending to take dishonestly any movable property out of the possession of any person without that person's consent, moves that property in order to such taking, commits theft.
simpleExplanation: Theft occurs when someone dishonestly takes movable property belonging to another person without consent.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both. Second or subsequent conviction: rigorous imprisonment from 1 year up to 5 years and fine. For first-time theft involving property below ₹5,000, community service may be imposed if the property is restored or its value returned.
examples: Stealing a mobile phone from another person's possession.
Removing a valuable item from someone's house without permission.
Taking pledged property dishonestly from a lawful custodian.

faqs.question: Can a person's own property be stolen?
faqs.answer: Yes. If the property is lawfully in another person's possession and is taken dishonestly, it may amount to theft.

faqs.question: Is found property always theft?
faqs.answer: No. Property not in anyone's possession is generally not the subject of theft, though other offences may apply.

title: Snatching
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Theft is snatching when the offender suddenly, quickly, or forcibly seizes, grabs, secures, or takes away movable property from a person or from that person's possession.
simpleExplanation: Snatching is a special form of theft involving sudden or forceful grabbing of property.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
examples: Snatching a chain from a pedestrian.
Quickly grabbing a mobile phone from someone's hand and running away.

faqs.question: How is snatching different from ordinary theft?
faqs.answer: Snatching involves sudden or forceful taking directly from a person or their possession.

title: Theft in Dwelling House, Means of Transport or Place of Worship
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits theft in a dwelling house, building, tent, vessel, means of transport, from transport vehicles, of idols or icons from places of worship, or of Government or local authority property commits an aggravated form of theft.
simpleExplanation: Certain types of theft are treated more seriously because of the location or nature of the property stolen.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Stealing from a residence.
Stealing goods from a train or bus.
Stealing an idol from a temple.
Stealing Government property.

faqs.question: Why is this punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because the offence involves protected places, public property, or transportation systems.

title: Theft by Clerk or Servant
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being a clerk, servant, or employee acting in such capacity, commits theft of property in the possession of his master or employer commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Employees who steal property belonging to their employer are subject to enhanced punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: An office employee stealing company cash.
A domestic servant stealing valuables from an employer's house.

faqs.question: Does the property have to belong to the employer?
faqs.answer: The property must be in the possession of the employer at the time of theft.

title: Theft After Preparation Made for Causing Death, Hurt or Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits theft after making preparations for causing death, hurt, restraint, or fear of death, hurt, or restraint in order to commit the theft, escape after theft, or retain stolen property commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: The law imposes harsher punishment where the thief prepares to use violence or intimidation while committing theft.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Carrying a loaded weapon while planning a theft.
Keeping accomplices ready to restrain victims if resistance occurs.

faqs.question: Must violence actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation for causing death, hurt, restraint, or fear is sufficient.

title: Extortion
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever intentionally puts any person in fear of injury and thereby dishonestly induces that person to deliver property, valuable security, or a document capable of becoming a valuable security commits extortion.
simpleExplanation: Extortion occurs when someone obtains property or valuables by threatening another person.

punishment.extortion: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.attempt_to_cause_fear: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.fear_of_death_or_grievous_hurt: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.extortion_using_fear_of_death_or_grievou: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.fear_of_serious_criminal_accusation: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Threatening to reveal defamatory information unless money is paid.
Demanding money by threatening harm to a family member.
Threatening a person with a false criminal accusation to obtain property.

faqs.question: Is physical violence necessary for extortion?
faqs.answer: No. Fear of injury alone can be sufficient.

title: Robbery
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Robbery is theft or extortion accompanied by actual or threatened violence, instant hurt, instant death, or wrongful restraint.
simpleExplanation: Robbery is a more serious offence where theft or extortion is committed using immediate force, violence, or threats.

punishment.robbery: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.attempted_robbery: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.robbery_causing_hurt: Imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Using a weapon to force a person to hand over money.
Taking property after restraining a victim.
Extorting property through immediate threats of violence.

faqs.question: Is every theft a robbery?
faqs.answer: No. Theft becomes robbery only when accompanied by violence, restraint, or immediate threats.

title: Dacoity
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: When five or more persons conjointly commit or attempt to commit robbery, or aid such commission or attempt, every such person commits dacoity.
simpleExplanation: Dacoity is robbery committed by a group of five or more persons acting together.

punishment.dacoity: Imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.murder_during_dacoity: Death, or imprisonment for life, or rigorous imprisonment not less than 10 years, and fine.
punishment.preparation_for_dacoity: Rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.assembly_for_dacoity: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.membership_of_dacoity_gang: Imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: A gang of five persons jointly robbing a house.
Five persons assembling with weapons to commit a planned robbery.
A habitual gang organized for committing dacoities.

faqs.question: How many people are required for dacoity?
faqs.answer: At least five persons acting together.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished even if the robbery was not completed?
faqs.answer: Yes. Preparation, attempt, assembly, and gang membership are separately punishable.

title: Robbery or Dacoity with Attempt to Cause Death or Grievous Hurt
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: If, at the time of committing robbery or dacoity, the offender uses any deadly weapon, causes grievous hurt, or attempts to cause death or grievous hurt, enhanced punishment applies.
simpleExplanation: A robber or dacoit who uses a deadly weapon or causes or attempts serious injury faces a mandatory minimum punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment of not less than 7 years.
examples: Using a knife during a robbery.
Causing grievous injuries while committing dacoity.

faqs.question: Is actual grievous hurt necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Even an attempt to cause death or grievous hurt attracts this section.

title: Attempt to Commit Robbery or Dacoity when Armed with Deadly Weapon
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever attempts to commit robbery or dacoity while armed with a deadly weapon is liable under this section.
simpleExplanation: Even if robbery or dacoity is not completed, being armed with a deadly weapon during the attempt attracts severe punishment.
punishment: Imprisonment of not less than 7 years.
examples: Attempting a bank robbery while carrying a firearm.
Being caught while attempting dacoity with deadly weapons.

faqs.question: Must the weapon be used?
faqs.answer: No. Being armed with a deadly weapon during the attempt is sufficient.

title: Punishment for Belonging to Gang of Robbers or Thieves
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever belongs to a gang habitually associated for committing theft or robbery, but not dacoity, commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Membership in a gang that regularly commits thefts or robberies is itself punishable.
punishment: Rigorous imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Being part of an organized gang specializing in robberies.
Habitually participating in a theft syndicate.

faqs.question: Must a specific theft be proved?
faqs.answer: The prosecution must establish habitual association with a gang committing theft or robbery.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates or converts movable property to his own use commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Keeping or using someone else's property dishonestly for personal benefit amounts to criminal misappropriation.
punishment: Imprisonment from 6 months up to 2 years and fine.
examples: Finding a lost wallet, identifying the owner, and keeping it.
Selling a borrowed item for personal gain.

faqs.question: Is every finder of lost property guilty?
faqs.answer: No. A finder who genuinely attempts to locate the owner is not guilty.

title: Dishonest Misappropriation of Property Possessed by Deceased Person
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly misappropriates property that was in the possession of a deceased person and has not yet come into the possession of a lawful heir commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: Taking property belonging to a deceased person before it reaches the rightful owner is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine; up to 7 years if committed by the deceased person's clerk or servant.
examples: A servant stealing money belonging to a deceased employer before heirs receive it.

faqs.question: Does the property need to be unclaimed?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section applies before the property comes into lawful possession of an entitled person.

title: Criminal Breach of Trust
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever, being entrusted with property or dominion over property, dishonestly misappropriates, converts, uses, or disposes of it in violation of law or contractual obligations commits criminal breach of trust.
simpleExplanation: A person who is entrusted with property and dishonestly uses it for personal benefit commits criminal breach of trust.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.carrier_wharfinger_warehouse_keeper: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.clerk_or_servant: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.public_servant_banker_merchant_agent: Imprisonment for life or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: An employee diverting entrusted funds for personal use.
A warehouse keeper selling stored goods without authority.
A public servant misusing government funds.

faqs.question: Is mere negligence enough?
faqs.answer: No. Dishonest intention is essential.

title: Stolen Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Property obtained through theft, extortion, robbery, cheating, criminal misappropriation, or criminal breach of trust is designated as stolen property.
simpleExplanation: Receiving, retaining, concealing, or dealing in property known to be stolen is punishable.

punishment.receiving_stolen_property: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.receiving_property_from_dacoity: Imprisonment for life or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.habitual_dealing_in_stolen_property: Imprisonment for life or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.assisting_concealment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples: Buying a stolen mobile phone knowing it was stolen.
Habitually trading in stolen goods.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished for merely retaining stolen property?
faqs.answer: Yes, if they knew or had reason to believe it was stolen.

title: Cheating
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever deceives another person and thereby dishonestly or fraudulently induces delivery of property or causes harm through such deception commits cheating.
simpleExplanation: Cheating occurs when someone deceives another person to gain property, benefits, or cause harm.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.breach_of_protective_duty: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.cheating_inducing_delivery_of_property: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

examples: Obtaining money through false promises.
Selling counterfeit goods as genuine products.
Concealing material facts to induce a transaction.

faqs.question: Can concealment amount to cheating?
faqs.answer: Yes. Dishonest concealment of facts is considered deception.

title: Cheating by Personation
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: A person cheats by personation when he pretends to be another person or falsely represents himself or another person.
simpleExplanation: Impersonating another person to deceive someone is cheating by personation.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Pretending to be a bank officer to obtain money.
Using the identity of a deceased person to deceive others.

faqs.question: Can an imaginary identity amount to personation?
faqs.answer: Yes. The offence applies whether the person impersonated is real or imaginary.

title: Fraudulent Removal or Transfer of Property to Defeat Creditors
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently removes, conceals, delivers, or transfers property without adequate consideration to prevent lawful distribution among creditors commits an offence.
simpleExplanation: A debtor cannot hide or transfer assets dishonestly to avoid paying creditors.
punishment: Imprisonment from 6 months up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Transferring assets to relatives to avoid debt recovery.
Concealing property during insolvency proceedings.

faqs.question: Does every transfer of property attract this section?
faqs.answer: No. The transfer must be dishonest or fraudulent and intended to defeat creditors.

title: Dishonestly or Fraudulently Preventing Debt Being Available for Creditors
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
category: Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions of Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently prevents any debt or demand due to himself or to any other person from being made available according to law for payment of his debts or the debts of such other person.
simpleExplanation: A person commits this offence when they deliberately hide, transfer, waive, or otherwise prevent money owed to them from being used to pay creditors.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A transfers a debt owed to him to a friend to prevent creditors from recovering it.
A fraudulently waives a valid claim so creditors cannot access the money.

faqs.question: What is the purpose of this section?
faqs.answer: To prevent debtors from defeating the lawful claims of creditors.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Execution of Deed of Transfer Containing False Statement of Consideration
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
category: Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions of Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently signs, executes or becomes a party to any deed or instrument purporting to transfer or charge property and containing false statements regarding consideration or beneficial ownership.
simpleExplanation: It is an offence to participate in property transfer documents that contain false information about payment, ownership, or beneficiaries.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A property sale deed falsely states a lower sale price to evade liabilities.
A transfer document falsely names a beneficiary to conceal the real owner.

faqs.question: Does this apply to fake property sale agreements?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the document contains false statements about consideration or ownership.

title: Dishonest or Fraudulent Removal or Concealment of Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
category: Fraudulent Deeds and Dispositions of Property
officialText: Whoever dishonestly or fraudulently conceals or removes property of himself or another person, assists in such concealment or removal, or dishonestly releases any claim to which he is entitled.
simpleExplanation: Hiding property, helping others hide property, or giving up legitimate claims to defeat legal rights is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: A hides assets before a court-ordered recovery process.
A helps a debtor move assets to avoid attachment by creditors.

faqs.question: Can helping someone conceal property be an offence?
faqs.answer: Yes. Assisting in concealment or removal of property is also punishable.

title: Mischief
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever intentionally or knowingly causes wrongful loss or damage by destroying property, reducing its value or utility, or affecting it injuriously commits mischief.
simpleExplanation: Mischief means intentionally damaging, destroying, or reducing the usefulness or value of property.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 6 months, or fine, or both.
punishment.damage_to_property: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
punishment.damage_between_20000_and_100000: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.damage_above_100000: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.with_preparation_for_death_hurt_restrain: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.

examples: Destroying another person's vehicle.
Burning a document to cause financial loss.
Damaging crops by driving cattle into a field.

faqs.question: Can mischief be committed against one's own property?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the act causes wrongful loss or damage to another person.

title: Mischief by Killing or Maiming Animal
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief by killing, poisoning, maiming or rendering useless any animal.
simpleExplanation: Intentionally harming, poisoning, killing, or disabling an animal is a specific form of mischief.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Poisoning a neighbour's livestock.
Maiming a working animal to cause loss to its owner.

faqs.question: Does this apply only to livestock?
faqs.answer: No. It applies to any animal harmed through acts constituting mischief.

title: Mischief by Injury, Inundation, Fire or Explosive Substance
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Punishes special forms of mischief involving water supply, roads, navigation, drainage, landmarks, fire, and explosive substances.
simpleExplanation: This section covers serious forms of property damage affecting public infrastructure, water resources, navigation systems, or buildings.

punishment.water_supply_damage: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.road_bridge_navigation_damage: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.drainage_or_inundation_damage: Imprisonment up to 5 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.navigation_signal_damage: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.landmark_damage: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
punishment.fire_or_explosive_damage_to_property: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.fire_or_explosive_damage_to_dwelling_or_: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Damaging a bridge to make it unsafe.
Destroying a railway navigation signal.
Setting fire to a warehouse.

faqs.question: Why is damage to navigation signals punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because it can endanger public safety and transportation systems.

title: Mischief with Intent to Destroy or Make Unsafe a Rail, Aircraft or Vessel
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits mischief intending to destroy or render unsafe any rail, aircraft, decked vessel, or vessel of twenty tons burden or more.
simpleExplanation: Damaging transportation systems such as railways, aircraft, or large vessels is treated as a grave offence.

punishment.destroy_or_render_unsafe: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.using_fire_or_explosives: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Sabotaging a railway track.
Damaging an aircraft to make it unsafe for operation.

faqs.question: Does an attempt attract punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Attempts using fire or explosives are specifically punishable.

title: Intentionally Running Vessel Aground or Ashore with Intent to Commit Theft
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever intentionally runs a vessel aground or ashore intending to commit theft or dishonest misappropriation of property contained therein.
simpleExplanation: Purposely causing a vessel to run aground in order to steal or misappropriate cargo is an offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
examples: Deliberately grounding a ship to steal cargo.
Causing a vessel to strand so that goods can be unlawfully taken.

faqs.question: Is actual theft necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Intent to facilitate theft or misappropriation is sufficient.

title: Criminal Trespass and House-Trespass
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Entering property in another's possession with intent to commit an offence, intimidate, insult or annoy, or unlawfully remaining there with such intent constitutes criminal trespass. Entry into a dwelling, place of worship, or place of custody of property constitutes house-trespass.
simpleExplanation: Unauthorized entry onto another's property with wrongful intent is criminal trespass. Entering homes or similar places is house-trespass.

punishment.criminal_trespass: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
punishment.house_trespass: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.

examples: Entering a property to intimidate the owner.
Remaining in a building after being ordered to leave with intent to annoy occupants.

faqs.question: Can partial entry amount to house-trespass?
faqs.answer: Yes. Introducing any part of the body is sufficient.

title: Lurking House-Trespass and House-Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: House-trespass committed while concealing it from persons entitled to exclude the trespasser is lurking house-trespass. House-breaking occurs when entry or exit is effected through specified unlawful methods.
simpleExplanation: Secret house-trespass is called lurking house-trespass. Entering or leaving a house through unlawful means such as breaking locks, climbing walls, or making openings amounts to house-breaking.
punishment: Definitions section; punishment is provided in subsequent sections.
examples: Entering through a hole made in a wall.
Opening a locked door to enter unlawfully.
Climbing through a window to commit an offence.

faqs.question: What is the difference between house-trespass and house-breaking?
faqs.answer: House-breaking requires entry or exit through one of the specific unlawful methods described in the section.

title: Punishment for Lurking House-Trespass or House-Breaking
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Prescribes punishments for lurking house-trespass or house-breaking, including aggravated forms committed at night, for committing other offences, with preparation for violence, or causing grievous hurt.
simpleExplanation: This section punishes secret house-trespass or house-breaking, with harsher penalties when committed at night, to commit another crime, or when violence is involved.

punishment.basic_offence: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.
punishment.after_sunset_before_sunrise: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
punishment.to_commit_other_imprisonable_offence: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine; if theft intended, up to 10 years.
punishment.night_time_for_other_offence: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine; if theft intended, up to 14 years.
punishment.with_preparation_for_hurt_or_assault: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.night_time_with_preparation_for_hurt: Imprisonment up to 14 years and fine.
punishment.causing_grievous_hurt_or_attempting_deat: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.joint_liability_night_time_grievous_hurt: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.

examples: Breaking into a house at night to commit theft.
Entering a house secretly with weapons to threaten occupants.

faqs.question: Why is night-time house-breaking punished more severely?
faqs.answer: Because it creates greater danger and fear for occupants.

title: House-Trespass in Order to Commit Offence
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Punishes house-trespass committed for the purpose of committing offences punishable with death, life imprisonment, or other imprisonment.
simpleExplanation: Entering a house unlawfully with the intention of committing another crime attracts enhanced punishment based on the seriousness of the intended offence.

punishment.offence_punishable_with_death: Life imprisonment or rigorous imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.offence_punishable_with_life_imprisonmen: Imprisonment up to 10 years and fine.
punishment.other_imprisonable_offence: Imprisonment up to 2 years and fine.

examples: Entering a house intending to commit murder.
House-trespass committed to carry out a theft.

faqs.question: Does the intended offence matter?
faqs.answer: Yes. Punishment depends on the gravity of the offence intended.

title: House-Trespass After Preparation for Hurt, Assault or Wrongful Restraint
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Whoever commits house-trespass after making preparation for causing hurt, assault, wrongful restraint, or fear thereof.
simpleExplanation: Entering a house unlawfully after preparing to use violence or intimidation is treated as a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Entering a house carrying weapons to threaten occupants.
House-trespass with preparation to restrain residents.

faqs.question: Is actual violence necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Preparation for violence is sufficient.

title: Dishonestly Breaking Open Receptacle Containing Property
chapter: Chapter XVII - Of Offences Against Property
officialText: Punishes breaking open or unfastening a closed receptacle containing property dishonestly or with intent to commit mischief.
simpleExplanation: Opening locked boxes, safes, containers, or similar receptacles without authority for dishonest purposes is punishable.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.entrusted_receptacle: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.

examples: Breaking open a locked safe to access valuables.
A custodian opening a sealed container entrusted to him without authority.

faqs.question: Does this apply to entrusted property?
faqs.answer: Yes. Punishment is higher when the receptacle was entrusted to the offender.

title: Making a False Document or False Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Defines when a person is said to make a false document or false electronic record by creating, altering, signing, executing, transmitting, or causing execution of documents or electronic records dishonestly or fraudulently.
simpleExplanation: This section defines what constitutes a false document or electronic record, forming the foundation of forgery offences.
punishment: Definition section; punishment provided in subsequent sections.
examples: Signing another person's name without authority.
Altering a will or cheque after execution.
Creating a fake electronic record.

faqs.question: Can electronic records be forged?
faqs.answer: Yes. The section expressly covers electronic records and electronic signatures.

title: Forgery
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Whoever makes a false document or electronic record with intent to cause injury, support a claim, obtain property, induce a contract, or commit fraud commits forgery.
simpleExplanation: Creating or altering false documents or electronic records to deceive others or commit fraud is forgery.

punishment.general_forgery: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.for_purpose_of_cheating: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.to_harm_reputation: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

examples: Creating a fake property deed.
Forging a document to obtain a loan.
Producing a forged certificate to damage someone's reputation.

faqs.question: What is the difference between forgery and cheating?
faqs.answer: Forgery involves creating or altering false documents, while cheating involves deception causing wrongful loss or gain.

title: Forgery of Court Records, Public Registers and Identity Documents
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes forgery of court records, government identity documents, public registers, certificates, powers of attorney and similar official documents.
simpleExplanation: Forging important public, judicial, or identity documents is treated as a serious offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Forging an Aadhaar card.
Creating a fake court order.
Forging a power of attorney.

faqs.question: Does this cover digital government records?
faqs.answer: Yes. Electronic registers, records, and data are included.

title: Forgery of Valuable Security, Will or Authority
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes forgery of valuable securities, wills, authorities to adopt, powers relating to money or property, receipts, and similar documents.
simpleExplanation: Forging documents that directly affect money, property, inheritance, or financial rights is a very serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment, or imprisonment up to 10 years, and fine.
examples: Forging a will to inherit property.
Creating a fake share certificate.
Forging a receipt acknowledging payment.

faqs.question: Why is punishment higher under this section?
faqs.answer: Because forged valuable securities and wills can cause significant financial and legal harm.

title: Possession of Forged Document or Electronic Record
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes possession of forged documents or electronic records with knowledge that they are forged and intention to use them as genuine.
simpleExplanation: Keeping forged documents while intending to use them as genuine is itself an offence.

punishment.documents_under_section_337: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.documents_under_section_338: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

examples: Possessing a forged Aadhaar card for future use.
Holding a forged will knowing it is fake.

faqs.question: Is actual use necessary?
faqs.answer: No. Possession with knowledge and intent to use is sufficient.

title: Using Forged Document or Electronic Record as Genuine
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Defines forged documents and punishes anyone who fraudulently or dishonestly uses a forged document or electronic record as genuine.
simpleExplanation: Using a forged document while knowing or believing it to be forged is treated the same as forging it.
punishment: Same punishment as the forgery offence applicable to that document.
examples: Submitting a forged degree certificate for employment.
Using a fake court order to obtain benefits.

faqs.question: Can a person be punished even if they did not create the forged document?
faqs.answer: Yes. Using a forged document knowingly attracts the same punishment as forgery.

title: Making or Possessing Counterfeit Seal, Plate or Instrument for Forgery
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes making, counterfeiting, possessing, or using counterfeit seals, plates, or instruments intended for committing forgery.
simpleExplanation: Creating or possessing fake seals, stamps, dies, or similar tools used to forge important documents is a criminal offence.

punishment.for_forgery_under_section_338: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.for_other_forgery_offences: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.mere_possession_of_counterfeit_instrumen: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.
punishment.using_counterfeit_instrument_as_genuine: Same punishment as making or counterfeiting it.

examples: Manufacturing a fake government seal for forging wills.
Possessing a counterfeit stamp intended for creating forged certificates.

faqs.question: Is possession alone punishable?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the person knows the instrument is counterfeit.

title: Counterfeiting Device or Mark Used for Authenticating Documents
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes counterfeiting authentication devices, marks, or materials used to make forged documents appear genuine.
simpleExplanation: Making fake watermarks, authentication marks, security features, or possessing materials carrying such counterfeit marks is an offence.

punishment.for_documents_under_section_338: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
punishment.for_other_documents: Imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.

examples: Counterfeiting a security watermark used on valuable securities.
Creating fake authentication marks for official certificates.

faqs.question: Does possession of counterfeit-marked material attract punishment?
faqs.answer: Yes, when possessed with the required criminal intent.

title: Fraudulent Cancellation, Destruction or Secretion of Valuable Documents
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes fraudulent cancellation, destruction, defacement, concealment, or damage to wills, adoption authorities, and valuable securities.
simpleExplanation: Destroying, hiding, or damaging important legal or financial documents to cause loss or gain an advantage is a serious offence.
punishment: Life imprisonment or imprisonment up to 7 years and fine.
examples: Destroying a will to prevent inheritance.
Concealing a valuable security certificate.

faqs.question: What documents are specially protected?
faqs.answer: Wills, authorities to adopt, valuable securities, and similar documents.

title: Falsification of Accounts
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes employees, clerks, officers, or servants who falsify accounts, records, books, electronic records, or valuable securities with intent to defraud.
simpleExplanation: Employees who manipulate accounts, destroy records, make false entries, or alter important documents to commit fraud can be prosecuted under this section.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.
examples: An accountant making false entries in company books.
An employee deleting financial records to hide embezzlement.

faqs.question: Must a specific victim be identified?
faqs.answer: No. A general intent to defraud is sufficient.

title: Property Mark and Use of False Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Defines property marks and punishes the use of false property marks intended to misrepresent ownership of goods.
simpleExplanation: Using labels, marks, or symbols that falsely indicate ownership of goods is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Marking goods as belonging to another company.
Using false ownership labels on products.

faqs.question: What is a property mark?
faqs.answer: A mark used to show that movable property belongs to a particular person.

title: Removing, Destroying or Defacing Property Mark
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes removing, destroying, altering, or adding to a property mark with intent or knowledge that injury may result.
simpleExplanation: Tampering with ownership marks on goods to mislead others or cause harm is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Removing a manufacturer's ownership mark from machinery.
Altering product ownership labels.

faqs.question: Is intent important?
faqs.answer: Yes. The act must be done intending or knowing that injury is likely.

title: Counterfeiting Property Marks
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes counterfeiting property marks and official marks used by public servants.
simpleExplanation: Creating fake ownership marks or government certification marks is a criminal offence.

punishment.general_property_mark: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.public_servant_mark: Imprisonment up to 3 years and fine.

examples: Counterfeiting a company's trademark ownership mark.
Creating fake government quality certification marks.

faqs.question: Why are public servant marks treated more seriously?
faqs.answer: Because they are relied upon by the public and government authorities.

title: Making or Possessing Instruments for Counterfeiting Property Marks
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes making or possessing dies, plates, instruments, or property marks intended for counterfeiting ownership marks.
simpleExplanation: Possessing tools or fake ownership labels meant for counterfeiting goods is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Keeping printing plates for fake ownership labels.
Possessing counterfeit property marks for use on goods.

faqs.question: Must the counterfeit mark actually be used?
faqs.answer: No. Possession for the purpose of counterfeiting is sufficient.

title: Sale or Possession of Goods Bearing Counterfeit Property Marks
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes selling, exposing for sale, or possessing goods bearing counterfeit property marks unless statutory defenses are established.
simpleExplanation: Selling products carrying fake ownership or identifying marks is punishable unless the seller can prove innocence and due diligence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Selling goods with counterfeit ownership labels.
Offering products marked with fake manufacturer identifiers.

faqs.question: Can a seller avoid liability?
faqs.answer: Yes, by proving reasonable precautions, lack of knowledge, and innocent conduct.

title: False Marks on Cases, Packages or Receptacles
chapter: Chapter XVIII - Of Offences Relating to Documents and Property Marks
officialText: Punishes making or using false marks on packages or containers intended to misrepresent the contents, nature, or quality of goods.
simpleExplanation: Placing false labels on packages to mislead authorities or buyers about the contents or quality of goods is a criminal offence.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Labeling low-quality goods as premium products.
Marking a package as containing one product while it contains another.

faqs.question: Is use of a false mark punishable even if someone else created it?
faqs.answer: Yes. Using the false mark attracts the same punishment.

title: Criminal Intimidation
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Whoever threatens another with injury to person, reputation or property with intent to cause alarm or compel an act or omission commits criminal intimidation.
simpleExplanation: Threatening someone to frighten them or force them to do something they are not legally required to do is criminal intimidation.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.serious_threats: Imprisonment up to 7 years, or fine, or both.

examples: Threatening to burn a person's house if they file a lawsuit.
Sending anonymous threats demanding money.

faqs.question: Can threatening a deceased person's reputation amount to intimidation?
faqs.answer: Yes, if the threatened person has an interest in that deceased person.

title: Intentional Insult with Intent to Provoke Breach of Peace
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Punishes intentional insults likely to provoke a person to break public peace or commit an offence.
simpleExplanation: Deliberately insulting someone in a manner likely to trigger violence or disturbance of public peace is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
examples: Provoking a person with abusive language intending to start a fight.

faqs.question: Is every insult punishable?
faqs.answer: No. The insult must be intended or likely to provoke a breach of peace or another offence.

title: Statements Conducing to Public Mischief
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Punishes publication or circulation of false information, rumours or reports likely to cause mutiny, public alarm, public disorder, or communal hatred.
simpleExplanation: Spreading false rumours or misinformation that can create panic, violence, public disorder, or communal tension is an offence.

punishment.general: Imprisonment up to 3 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.place_of_worship: Imprisonment up to 5 years and fine.

examples: Spreading false rumours causing public panic.
Publishing false information to create hatred between communities.

faqs.question: Is good-faith publication protected?
faqs.answer: Yes. If there were reasonable grounds to believe the information was true and it was published in good faith.

title: Act Caused by Inducing Belief of Divine Displeasure
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Punishes inducing a person to act or refrain from acting by making them believe they will suffer divine displeasure.
simpleExplanation: Using religious fear or superstition to force someone into doing or not doing something is punishable.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 1 year, or fine, or both.
examples: Threatening someone with divine punishment unless they comply with a demand.
Using a dharna to make a person believe they will suffer divine displeasure.

faqs.question: What is the essence of this offence?
faqs.answer: Coercion through fear of supernatural or divine consequences.

title: Misconduct in Public by a Drunken Person
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Punishes intoxicated persons who cause annoyance in public places or places where their presence amounts to trespass.
simpleExplanation: Being drunk in public and behaving in a way that annoys others is an offence.
punishment: Simple imprisonment up to 24 hours, or fine up to ₹1,000, or both, or community service.
examples: Creating a disturbance while intoxicated in a public place.

faqs.question: Can community service be imposed?
faqs.answer: Yes, community service is specifically provided as a punishment.

title: Defamation
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Punishes making or publishing imputations harming the reputation of a person, subject to statutory exceptions.
simpleExplanation: Making false statements or allegations that damage another person's reputation may amount to defamation.

punishment.defamation: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both, or community service.
punishment.printing_defamatory_matter: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.
punishment.selling_defamatory_matter: Simple imprisonment up to 2 years, or fine, or both.

examples: Publishing false allegations damaging a person's reputation.
Printing defamatory content knowing it to be defamatory.

faqs.question: Are there exceptions to defamation?
faqs.answer: Yes. Truth for public good, good-faith criticism, court reports, fair comment, and several other exceptions are recognized.

faqs.question: Can a deceased person be defamed?
faqs.answer: Yes, under certain circumstances affecting family or relatives.

title: Breach of Contract to Attend on and Supply Wants of Helpless Person
chapter: Chapter XIX - Of Criminal Intimidation, Insult, Annoyance, Defamation, etc.
officialText: Punishes a person who, being bound by contract to care for a helpless person, voluntarily omits to do so.
simpleExplanation: A person contractually responsible for caring for a helpless individual commits an offence if they deliberately neglect that responsibility.
punishment: Imprisonment up to 3 months, or fine up to ₹5,000, or both.
examples: A contracted caregiver deliberately abandoning a helpless patient.

faqs.question: Who is considered helpless?
faqs.answer: A person unable to care for themselves because of youth, unsoundness of mind, disease, or bodily weakness.

title: Repeal and Savings
chapter: Chapter XX - Repeal and Savings
officialText: Repeals the Indian Penal Code, 1860 and preserves rights, liabilities, penalties, investigations and proceedings arising under the repealed law.
simpleExplanation: This section formally repeals the IPC while ensuring that actions, rights, liabilities, investigations and proceedings under the IPC continue to remain valid.
punishment: Not applicable.
examples: A criminal case initiated under IPC before repeal can continue under the saving provisions.

faqs.question: Does repeal of IPC cancel pending cases?
faqs.answer: No. Existing investigations, prosecutions, liabilities and penalties continue unaffected.

faqs.question: What happens to actions already taken under IPC?
faqs.answer: They are deemed to have been taken under the corresponding provisions of the Bharatiya Nyaya Sanhita.
